templates.name: android_aflags
templates.description: Android aconfig flags ("aflags") are a configuration system used to manage feature rollout and behavior across the Android platform. For more details on aconfig, see: https://source.android.com/docs/setup/build/feature-flagging/declare-flag This view presents snapshots of the state of these flags as captured during the trace. Data is collected by the `android.aflags` data-source, which periodically polls the `aflags` tool on Android devices. Each row in this view represents the state of a single flag at a specific point in time (the `ts` column).

templates.name: _default_anr_dur
templates.description: Function to get the default ANR duration in milliseconds based on ANR type. Note: These are common defaults. Actual timeouts can vary by OEM, Android version, and FG/BG status.

templates.name: _extract_anr_duration_from_subject
templates.description: Extract the ANR duration (milliseconds) from the subject line

templates.name: _platform_to_standard_anr_type
templates.description: Some of the anr timer events don't use the standard anr types and we have to convert them (temporal solution). For 'JobScheduler' there's not a 1:1 mapping to a standard type.

templates.name: android_anrs
templates.description: List of all ANRs that occurred in the trace (one row per ANR).

templates.name: _cold_start
templates.description: Broadcast, service and activity cold starts.

templates.name: _cold_proc_start
templates.description: Join Broadcast, service and activity cold starts with process starts.

templates.name: _provider_proc_start
templates.description: Join Provider cold starts with process starts.

templates.name: android_app_process_starts
templates.description: All app cold starts with information about their cold start reason: broadcast, service, activity or provider.

templates.name: android_auto_multiuser_timing
templates.description: Time elapsed between the latest user start and the specific end event like package startup(ex carlauncher) or previous user stop.

templates.name: android_auto_multiuser_timing_with_previous_user_resource_usage
templates.description: This table extends `android_auto_multiuser_timing` table with previous user resource usage.

templates.name: android_light_idle_state
templates.description: Light idle states. This is the state machine that quickly detects the device is unused and restricts background activity. See https://developer.android.com/training/monitoring-device-state/doze-standby

templates.name: android_deep_idle_state
templates.description: Deep idle states. This is the state machine that more slowly detects deeper levels of device unuse and restricts background activity further. See https://developer.android.com/training/monitoring-device-state/doze-standby

templates.name: android_battery_stats_counter_to_string
templates.description: Converts a battery_stats counter value to human readable string.

templates.name: android_battery_stats_state
templates.description: View of human readable battery stats counter-based states. These are recorded by BatteryStats as a bitmap where each 'category' has a unique value at any given time.

templates.name: android_battery_stats_event_slices
templates.description: View of slices derived from battery_stats events. Battery stats records all events as instants, however some may indicate whether something started or stopped with a '+' or '-' prefix. Events such as jobs, top apps, foreground apps or long wakes include these details and allow drawing slices between instant events found in a trace. For example, we may see an event like the following on 'battery_stats.top': -top=10215:"com.google.android.apps.nexuslauncher" This view will find the associated start ('+top') with the matching suffix (everything after the '=') to construct a slice. It computes the timestamp and duration from the events and extract the details as follows: track_name='battery_stats.top' str_value='com.google.android.apps.nexuslauncher' int_value=10215

templates.name: _sync_binder_metrics_by_txn
templates.description: Breakdown synchronous binder transactions per txn. It returns data about the client and server ends of every binder transaction.

templates.name: android_sync_binder_thread_state_by_txn
templates.description: Aggregated thread_states on the client and server side per binder txn This builds on the data from |_sync_binder_metrics_by_txn| and for each end (client and server) of the transaction, it returns the aggregated sum of all the thread state durations. The |thread_state_type| column represents whether a given 'aggregated thread_state' row is on the client or server side. 'binder_txn' is client side and 'binder_reply' is server side.

templates.name: android_sync_binder_blocked_functions_by_txn
templates.description: Aggregated blocked_functions on the client and server side per binder txn This builds on the data from |_sync_binder_metrics_by_txn| and for each end (client and server) of the transaction, it returns the aggregated sum of all the kernel blocked function durations. The |thread_state_type| column represents whether a given 'aggregated blocked_function' row is on the client or server side. 'binder_txn' is client side and 'binder_reply' is server side.

templates.name: _async_binder_metrics_by_txn
templates.description: Breakdown asynchronous binder transactions per txn. It returns data about the client and server ends of every binder transaction async.

templates.name: android_binder_txns
templates.description: Breakdown binder transactions per txn. It returns data about the client and server ends of every binder transaction async.

templates.name: android_binder_outgoing_graph
templates.description: Returns a DAG of all outgoing binder txns from a process. The roots of the graph are the threads making the txns and the graph flows from: thread -> server_process -> AIDL interface -> AIDL method. The weights of each node represent the wall execution time in the server_process.

templates.name: android_binder_incoming_graph
templates.description: Returns a DAG of all incoming binder txns from a process. The roots of the graph are the clients making the txns and the graph flows from: client_process -> AIDL interface -> AIDL method. The weights of each node represent the wall execution time in the server_process.

templates.name: android_binder_graph
templates.description: Returns a graph of all binder txns in a trace. The nodes are client_process and server_process. The weights of each node represent the wall execution time in the server_process.

templates.name: _binder_client_view
templates.description: Client side of all binder txns, sorted by client_ts. Suitable for interval_intersect.

templates.name: _binder_server_view
templates.description: Server side of all binder txns, sorted by server_ts. Suitable for interval_intersect.

templates.name: _thread_state_view
templates.description: Thread state view suitable for interval intersect.

templates.name: _binder_flatten_descendants
templates.description: Partitions and flattens slices underneath the server or client side of binder txns. The |name| column in the output is the lowest depth slice name in a given partition.

templates.name: _binder_server_flat_descendants_with_thread_state
templates.description: Server side flattened descendants intersected with their thread_states.

templates.name: _binder_client_flat_descendants_with_thread_state
templates.description: Client side flattened descendants intersected with their thread_states.

templates.name: _binder_reason
templates.description: Returns the 'reason' for a binder txn delay based on the descendant slice name and thread_state information. It follows the following priority: 1. direct_reclaim. 2. GC blocking stall. 3. Sleeping in monitor contention. 4. Sleeping in ART lock contention. 5. Sleeping in binder txn or reply. 6. Sleeping in Mutex contention. 7. IO. 8. State itself.

templates.name: android_binder_server_breakdown
templates.description: Server side binder breakdowns per transactions per txn.

templates.name: android_binder_client_breakdown
templates.description: Client side binder breakdowns per transactions per txn.

templates.name: android_binder_client_server_breakdown
templates.description: Combined client and server side binder breakdowns per transaction.

templates.name: android_bitmap_memory
templates.description: Provides a timeseries of "Bitmap Memory" counter for each process, which is useful for retrieving the total memory used by bitmaps by an application over time. To populate this table, tracing must be enabled with the "view" atrace category.

templates.name: android_bitmap_count
templates.description: Provides a timeseries of "Bitmap Count" counter for each process, which is useful for retrieving the number of bitmaps used by an application over time. To populate this table, tracing must be enabled with the "view" atrace category.

templates.name: android_bitmap_counters_per_process
templates.description: Provides a timeseries of bitmap-related counters for each process, which is useful for understanding an application's bitmap usage over time. To populate this table, tracing must be enabled with the "view" atrace category.

templates.name: _android_broadcasts_minsdk_u
templates.description: Provides a list of broadcast names and processes they were sent to by the system_server process on U+ devices.

templates.name: android_cpu_cluster_mapping
templates.description: Stores the mapping of a cpu to its cluster type - e.g. little, medium, big. This cluster type is determined by initially using cpu_capacity from sysfs and grouping clusters with identical capacities, ordered by size. In the case that capacities are not present, max frequency is used instead. If nothing is avaiable, NULL is returned.

templates.name: _active_cpu_count_for_cluster_type
templates.description: The count of active CPUs with a given cluster type over time.

templates.name: android_cpu_per_uid_track
templates.description: Table of tracks for CPU-per-UID data. Each row represents one UID / cluster combination.

templates.name: android_cpu_per_uid_counter
templates.description: View of counters for CPU-per-UID data. Each row represents one instant in time for one UID / cluster.

templates.name: _android_critical_blocking_calls
templates.description: Extract critical blocking calls from all processes.

templates.name: _is_jank_slice
templates.description: Macro defining the filtering conditions for a jank or latency CUJ slice in relevant processes.

templates.name: _jank_cujs_slices
templates.description: List of CUJ slices emitted. Note that this is not the final list of CUJs with the correct boundary information. The proper CUJs and their boundaries are computed after taking into account instant events and frame boundaries in the following tables.

templates.name: _cuj_state_markers
templates.description: Slices logged from FrameTracker#markEvent that describe when the instrumentation was started and the reason the CUJ ended.

templates.name: _all_frames_in_cuj
templates.description: Information about all frames in a process that overlap with a CUJ from the same process. This can include multiple frames for the same frame_id (for eg. frames with different layers).

templates.name: _android_distinct_frames_in_cuj
templates.description: Track all distinct frames that overlap with the CUJ slice. In this table two frames are considered distinct if they have different frame_id/vsync.

templates.name: _android_distinct_frames_layers_cuj
templates.description: Track all distinct frames with layer_id consideration that overlap with the CUJ slice.

templates.name: _android_jank_cuj_do_frames
templates.description: Table captures all Choreographer#doFrame within a CUJ boundary.

templates.name: android_jank_cuj
templates.description: Table tracking all jank CUJs information.

templates.name: _marker_missed_callback
templates.description: A missed callback in a perfetto trace means the ftrace was unable to capture callback events for an operation. For eg. a SFMissedCallback indicates that events related to surfaceflinger operations, such as vsync synchronization, transaction/presentation callbacks were not captured. This view captures all such missed callback slices.

templates.name: _android_cuj_missed_vsyncs_for_callback
templates.description: Extract count of a given missed callback for a specific CUJ.

templates.name: _android_jank_cuj_counter
templates.description: Extract all counters for Jank CUJ tracks.

templates.name: _android_jank_cuj_counter_value
templates.description: Returns the counter value for the given CUJ name and counter name.

templates.name: android_sysui_jank_cujs
templates.description: Table tracking all jank CUJs information.

templates.name: _latency_cuj_tracks
templates.description: Track IDs for all tracks named after Latency CUJs.

templates.name: _latency_cuj_markers
templates.description: Markers (cancel, timeout) related to Latency CUJ slices.

templates.name: android_sysui_latency_cujs
templates.description: Table tracking all latency CUJs information.

templates.name: android_jank_latency_cujs
templates.description: Table tracking all jank/latency CUJs information.

templates.name: android_jank_cuj_app_thread
templates.description: Returns a table with all CUJs and an additional column for the track id of thread_name passed as parameter, if present in the same process of the cuj.

templates.name: android_jank_cuj_render_thread
templates.description: Table captures thread information for 'RenderThread' for all CUJs.

templates.name: android_desktop_mode_windows
templates.description: Desktop Windows with durations they were open.

templates.name: android_device_name
templates.description: Extract name of the device based on metadata from the trace.

templates.name: android_dumpsys_show_map
templates.description: This table represents memory mapping information from /proc/[pid]/smaps All memory values are in kilobytes (KB)

templates.name: android_dvfs_counter_stats
templates.description: Aggregates dvfs counter slice for statistic.

templates.name: android_dvfs_counter_residency
templates.description: Aggregates dvfs counter slice for residency

templates.name: android_entity_state_residency
templates.description: Android entity state residency samples. For details see: https://perfetto.dev/docs/reference/trace-config-proto#AndroidPowerConfig

templates.name: _extended_frame_boundary
templates.description: For cases when a blocking call starts within a frame, but does not end before the actual frame ends, a part of the blocking call can be missed while calculating the metric. To avoid this issue, the frame boundary is extended, spanning from the start of the current actual frame, till the start of either next actual frame or the next doFrame slice - whichever is earliest; we are relying on both types of slices to get more stable metrics for cases where, for instance, doFrame blocks are running without any visible changes on the screen - leading to no actual frames being drawn. When this happens there might be gaps in actual frame timeline, so then the next doFrame would be resolved as an earlier boundary for an extended frame.

templates.name: _blocking_calls_frame_cuj
templates.description: Capture blocking call duration within frames within a CUJ.

templates.name: android_is_sf_jank_type
templates.description: Categorizes whether the jank was caused by Surface Flinger

templates.name: android_is_app_jank_type
templates.description: Categorizes whether the jank was caused by the app

templates.name: android_is_missed_frame_type
templates.description: Categorizes whether the jank was caused by the sf, app or "Dropped Frame"

templates.name: _frame_id_count_in_actual_timeline
templates.description: `actual_frame_timeline_slice` returns the same slice name for different layer IDs or tracks. This happens because there can be multiple slices associated with a single frame_id. We are interested in unique names(frame_id) and the respective counts. This is a legacy table which is moved to this file because the android_frames_overrun table depends on it.

templates.name: _frame_id_count_in_expected_timeline
templates.description: `expected_frame_timeline_slice` returns the same slice name for different tracks. This happens because there can be multiple slices associated with a single frame_id. We are interested in unique names(frame_id) and the respective counts. This is a legacy table which is moved to this file because the android_frames_overrun table depends on it.

templates.name: android_frames_overrun
templates.description: The amount by which each frame missed of hit its deadline. Negative if the deadline was not missed. Frames are considered janky if `overrun` is positive. Calculated as the difference between the end of the `expected_frame_timeline_slice` and `actual_frame_timeline_slice` for the frame. Availability: from S (API 31). For Googlers: more details in go/android-performance-metrics-glossary.

templates.name: android_frames_ui_time
templates.description: How much time did the frame's Choreographer callbacks take.

templates.name: android_app_vsync_delay_per_frame
templates.description: App Vsync delay for a frame. The time between the VSYNC-app signal and the start of Choreographer work. Calculated as time difference between the actual frame start (from `actual_frame_timeline_slice`) and start of the `Choreographer#doFrame` slice. For Googlers: more details in go/android-performance-metrics-glossary.

templates.name: android_cpu_time_per_frame
templates.description: How much time did the frame take across the UI Thread + RenderThread. Calculated as sum of `app VSYNC delay` `Choreographer#doFrame` slice duration and summed durations of all `DrawFrame` slices associated with this frame. Availability: from N (API 24). For Googlers: more details in go/android-performance-metrics-glossary.

templates.name: _cpu_time_per_frame_fallback
templates.description: CPU time of frames which don't have `android_cpu_time_per_frame` available. Calculated as UI time of the frame + 5ms. For Googlers: more details in go/android-performance-metrics-glossary.

templates.name: android_frame_stats
templates.description: Aggregated stats of the frame. For Googlers: more details in go/android-performance-metrics-glossary.

templates.name: _get_frame_table_with_id
templates.description: Parses the slice name to fetch `frame_id` from `slice` table. Use with caution. Slice names are a flaky source of ids and the resulting table might require some further operations.

templates.name: android_frames_choreographer_do_frame
templates.description: All of the `Choreographer#doFrame` slices with their frame id.

templates.name: android_frames_draw_frame
templates.description: All of the `DrawFrame` slices with their frame id and render thread. There might be multiple DrawFrames slices for a single vsync (frame id). This happens when we are drawing multiple layers (e.g. status bar and notifications).

templates.name: _distinct_layer_actual_timeline_slice_per_process
templates.description: Fetch distinct actual frames per layer per process.

templates.name: _distinct_from_expected_timeline_slice_per_process
templates.description: Fetch distinct expected frames per process.

templates.name: _vsync_missed_callback
templates.description: Contains frames with missed SF or HWUI callbacks.

templates.name: android_frames_layers
templates.description: TODO(b/384322064) Match actual timeline slice with correct draw frame using layer name. All slices related to one frame. Aggregates `Choreographer#doFrame`, `actual_frame_timeline_slice` and `expected_frame_timeline_slice` slices. This table differs slightly from the android_frames table, as it captures the layer_id for each actual timeline slice too.

templates.name: android_frames
templates.description: Table based on the android_frames_layers table. It aggregates time, duration and counts information across different layers for a given frame_id in a given process.

templates.name: android_first_frame_after
templates.description: Returns first frame after the provided timestamp. The returning table has at most one row.

templates.name: _frames_maxsdk_28
templates.description: All slices related to one frame for max SDK 28. Aggregates "Choreographer#doFrame" and "DrawFrame". Tries to guess the `ts` and `dur` of the frame by first guessing which "DrawFrame" slices are related to which "Choreographer#doSlice".

templates.name: _pid_to_upid
templates.description: Converts a pid to a upid using the timestamp of occurrence of an event from |pid| to disambiguate duplicate pids. This is still best effort because it relies on having information about process start and end in the trace. In the edge case that we are missing this, it best effort returns the last upid.

templates.name: _translate_unfreeze_reason
templates.description: Translate unfreeze reason from INT to STRING. See: frameworks/proto_logging/stats/atoms.proto

templates.name: android_freezer_events
templates.description: All frozen processes and their frozen duration.

templates.name: _gc_slice
templates.description: Collect all GC slices. There's typically one enclosing slice but sometimes the CompactionPhase is outside the nesting and we need to include that.

templates.name: _gc_heap_counter
templates.description: Extract the heap counter into <ts, dur, upid>

templates.name: _gc_slice_with_final_heap
templates.description: Find the last heap counter after the GC slice dur. This is the best effort to find the final heap size after GC. The algorithm is like so: 1. Merge end_ts of the GC events with the start_ts of the heap counters. 2. Find the heap counter value right after each GC event.

templates.name: _gc_slice_heap
templates.description: Aggregate the min and max heap across the GC event, taking into account the last heap size derived earlier.

templates.name: android_garbage_collection_events
templates.description: All Garbage collection events with a breakdown of the time spent and heap reclaimed.

templates.name: _gc_stats_window
templates.description: A window of the trace to use for GC stats. We can't reliably use trace_dur(), because often it spans far outside the range of relevant data. Instead pick the window based on when we have 'Heap size (KB)' data available.

templates.name: _gc_heap_allocated
templates.description: Count heap allocations by summing positive changes to the 'Heap size (KB)' counter.

templates.name: _gc_during_android_startup
templates.description: Intersection of startup events and gcs, for understanding what GCs are happining during app startup.

templates.name: _gc_heap_utilization
templates.description: Estimate heap utilization across the trace. We weight the utilization by gc_period, which is meant to represent the time since the previous GC. We approximate gc_period as time to the start of the process or metrics utilization in case there is no previous GC for the process.

templates.name: _android_garbage_collection_process_stats
templates.description: Summary stats about how garbage collection is behaving for a process, including causes, costs and other information relevant for tuning the garbage collector.

templates.name: _android_garbage_collection_stats
templates.description: Summary stats about how garbage collection is behaving across the device, including causes, costs and other information relevant for tuning the garbage collector.

templates.name: android_mali_gpu_power_state
templates.description: GPU power state which is analogous to CPU idle state

templates.name: android_gpu_memory_per_process
templates.description: Counter for GPU memory per process with duration.

templates.name: android_gpu_work_period_track
templates.description: Tracks for GPU work period events originating from the `power/gpu_work_period` Linux ftrace tracepoint. This tracepoint is usually only available on selected Android devices.

templates.name: _input_event_frame_intersections
templates.description: Exact Match: Find the Choreographer#doFrame that directly interval intersects with the deliver event if it exists.

templates.name: _input_event_frame_speculative_matches
templates.description: Speculative Match: Find the immediate next frame for non-vsync-aligned events (e.g. unbatched events)

templates.name: android_input_events
templates.description: All input events with round trip latency breakdown. Input delivery is socket based and every input event sent from the OS needs to be ACK'ed by the app. This gives us 4 subevents to measure latencies between: 1. Input dispatch event sent from OS. 2. Input dispatch event received in app. 3. Input ACK event sent from app. 4. Input ACK event received in OS.

templates.name: android_key_events
templates.description: Key events processed by the Android framework (from android.input.inputevent data source).

templates.name: android_motion_events
templates.description: Motion events processed by the Android framework (from android.input.inputevent data source).

templates.name: android_input_event_dispatch
templates.description: Input event dispatching information in Android (from android.input.inputevent data source).

templates.name: _android_input_lifecycle_by_slice_id
templates.description: Retrieves the full lifecycle of an Android input event (Read -> Dispatch -> Receive -> Consume -> Frame) by matching a given slice ID from any stage of the pipeline.

templates.name: _android_io_f2fs_counter_stats
templates.description: Aggregates f2fs IO and latency stats by counter name.

templates.name: _android_io_f2fs_write_stats
templates.description: Aggregates f2fs_write stats by inode and thread.

templates.name: _android_io_f2fs_aggregate_write_stats
templates.description: Aggregates f2fs write stats. Counts distinct datapoints, total write operations, and bytes written

templates.name: android_job_scheduler_events
templates.description: All scheduled jobs and their latencies. The table is populated by ATrace using the system server ATrace category (`atrace_categories: "ss"`). You can also set the `atrace_apps` of interest. This differs from the `android_job_scheduler_states` table in the `android.job_scheduler_states` module which is populated by the `ScheduledJobStateChanged` atom. Using `android_job_scheduler_states` is preferred when the `ATOM_SCHEDULED_JOB_STATE_CHANGED` is available in the trace since it includes the constraint, screen, or charging state changes for each job in a trace.

templates.name: android_job_scheduler_states
templates.description: This table returns constraint changes that a job will go through in a single trace. Values in this table are derived from the the `ScheduledJobStateChanged` atom. This table differs from the `android_job_scheduler_with_screen_charging_states` in this module (`android.job_scheduler_states`) by only having job constraint information. See documentation for the `android_job_scheduler_with_screen_charging_states` for how tables in this module differ from `android_job_scheduler_events` table in the `android.job_scheduler` module and how to populate this table.

templates.name: android_job_scheduler_with_screen_charging_states
templates.description: This table returns the constraint, charging, and screen state changes that a job will go through in a single trace. Values from this table are derived from the `ScheduledJobStateChanged` atom. This differs from the `android_job_scheduler_events` table in the `android.job_scheduler` module which is derived from ATrace the system server category (`atrace_categories: "ss"`). This also differs from the `android_job_scheduler_states` in this module (`android.job_scheduler_states`) by providing charging and screen state changes. To populate this table, enable the Statsd Tracing Config with the ATOM_SCHEDULED_JOB_STATE_CHANGED push atom id. https://perfetto.dev/docs/reference/trace-config-proto#StatsdTracingConfig This table is preferred over `android_job_scheduler_events` since it contains more information and should be used whenever `ATOM_SCHEDULED_JOB_STATE_CHANGED` is available in a trace.

templates.name: android_kernel_wakelocks
templates.description: Table of kernel (or native) wakelocks with held duration. Subtracts suspended time from each period to calculate the fraction of awake time for which the wakelock was held.

templates.name: _attributed_dmabufs
templates.description: Match gralloc thread allocations to inbound binders

templates.name: android_dmabuf_allocs
templates.description: Track dmabuf allocations, re-attributing gralloc allocations to their source (if binder transactions to gralloc are recorded).

templates.name: android_memory_cumulative_dmabuf
templates.description: Provides a timeseries of dmabuf allocations for each process. To populate this table, tracing must be enabled with the "dmabuf_allocs" ftrace event.

templates.name: _android_bitmap_resolve_sender_upid
templates.description: Converts a pid to a upid using the timestamp of occurrence of an event from |pid| to disambiguate duplicate pids.

templates.name: _android_bitmap_storage_type_name
templates.description: Decodes a Bitmap PixelStorageType integer (0..3) into its enum name as declared in `frameworks/base/libs/hwui/hwui/Bitmap.h`. Returns NULL for inputs outside the known range.

templates.name: heap_graph_bitmaps
templates.description: Per-Bitmap-object view over the heap graph, with `mId` and `mSourceId` decoded into their pid / storage-type components and (for parcel-received Bitmaps) the sender resolved to a `upid` and process name at the heap dump's timestamp. One row per `android.graphics.Bitmap` instance per heap dump. Scalar columns derived from `heap_graph_primitive` (width/height/density/...) are populated only when the trace is an ART HPROF dump (`heap_graph_object_data` populated); on proto-format heap graphs (e.g. `android.java_hprof` data source) they are NULL and the row still appears so callers can fall back to size-only analysis.

templates.name: android_heap_graph_class_find_descendants
templates.description: Given a list of classes as ancestor classes, return all the classes that descend from them.

templates.name: android_heap_graph_class_summary_tree
templates.description: Table containing all the Android heap graphs in the trace converted to a shortest-path tree and then aggregated by class name. This table contains a "flamegraph-like" representation of the contents of the heap graph.

templates.name: _heap_graph_object_min_depth_tree
templates.description: Converts the heap graph into a tree by performing a BFS on the graph from the roots. This basically ends up with all paths being the shortest path from the root to the node (with lower ids being picked in the case of ties). Root nodes are sorted by class name before traversal to make the output more deterministic.

templates.name: heap_graph_dominator_tree
templates.description: All reachable heap graph objects, their immediate dominators and summary of their dominated sets. The heap graph dominator tree is calculated by stdlib graphs.dominator_tree. Each reachable object is a node in the dominator tree, their immediate dominator is their parent node in the tree, and their dominated set is all their descendants in the tree. All size information come from the heap_graph_object prelude table.

templates.name: android_heap_graph_class_aggregation
templates.description: Class-level breakdown of the java heap. Per type name aggregates the object stats and the dominator tree stats.

templates.name: _closest_value
templates.description: Returns either the value for the span matching ts exactly, or a data point up to 500ms in the future.

templates.name: android_heap_graph_stats
templates.description: Table summarizing java heap graphs collected with the ART perfetto module. Contains one row per heap graph, with summary statistics (e.g. total / reachable objects) and memory stats for the corresponding process at the time of the heap dump.

templates.name: _heap_graph_type_path_hash
templates.description: Given a table containing a "tree-ified" heap graph object table (i.e. by using a dominator tree or shortest path algorithm), computes a hash of the path from the root to each node in the graph based on class names. This allows an SQL aggregation of all nodes which have the same hash to build a "class-tree" instead of the object tree.

templates.name: _heap_graph_path_hash_aggregate
templates.description: Given a table containing heap graph tree-table with path hashes computed (see _heap_graph_type_path_hash macro), aggregates together all nodes with the same hash and also splits out "native size" as a separate node under the nodes which contain the native size.

templates.name: _heap_graph_path_hashes_to_class_tree
templates.description: Given a table containing heap graph tree-table aggregated by path hashes (see _heap_graph_path_hash_aggregate) computes the "class tree" by converting the path hashes to ids. Note that |tab| *must* be a Perfetto (e.g. not a subquery) for this macro to work.

templates.name: _heap_graph_object_tree_aggregation
templates.description: Aggregates cumulative sizes for each object in the heap graph tree. This performs an expensive bottom-up traversal of the tree to compute cumulative sizes, native sizes, and object counts for each node.

templates.name: _heap_graph_object_references_agg
templates.description: Returns object references with cumulative size information.

templates.name: _heap_graph_incoming_references_agg
templates.description: Returns incoming references with cumulative size information.

templates.name: _heap_graph_outgoing_references_agg
templates.description: Returns outgoing references with cumulative size information.

templates.name: android_heap_graph_java_oome_details
templates.description: Details of Java OutOfMemoryError exceptions that triggered heap dumps.

templates.name: _heap_graph_super_root_fn
templates.description: The assigned id of the "super root". Since a Java heap graph is a "forest" structure, we need to add a imaginary "super root" node which connects all the roots of the forest into a single connected component, so that the dominator tree algorithm can be performed.

templates.name: android_heap_profile_summary_tree
templates.description: Table summarising the amount of memory allocated by each callstack as seen by Android native heap profiling (i.e. profiling information collected by heapprofd). Note: this table collapses data from all processes together into a single table.

templates.name: _lmk_instant_events
templates.description: Kills recorded via the instant lowmemorykiller track Introduced with ag/32702401 (Mar 2025)

templates.name: _legacy_lmk_events
templates.description: LMK events based on the slice atrace event (legacy) Introduced with aosp/1782391 (Aug 2021)

templates.name: _kill_one_process_events
templates.description: The original lmkd trace events, deprecated in 2022

templates.name: android_process_memory_intervals
templates.description: A table of process memory counter intervals, clipped to the process lifetime. Provides a comprehensive view of memory usage, including raw values and zygote-adjusted values. NOTE: For 'mem.rss.anon', 'mem.swap', 'mem.rss.file', and 'mem.heap' tracks, we subtract the average memory usage of the zygote that forked the process (its parent). This provides a better estimate of the child process's unique memory usage by accounting for the baseline memory inherited from its zygote. This is a no-op for a process whose forking zygote has no memory samples in the trace (e.g. traces with only ftrace rss_stat, where idle zygotes are not sampled; process_stats polling captures them). NOTE: Some tracks may have a spike greater than 100MiB. This can be legitimate or an accounting issue: see b/418231246 for more details.

templates.name: _memory_breakdown_oom_intervals_prepared
templates.description: Create a table containing intervals of OOM adjustment scores. This table will be used as the right side of a span join.

templates.name: android_process_memory_intervals_by_oom_bucket
templates.description: Correlates memory counters with OOM adjustment scores. This table joins memory counters with OOM adjustment scores, providing insights into memory usage under system memory pressure. NOTE: For 'mem.rss.anon', 'mem.swap', 'mem.rss.file', and 'mem.heap' tracks, we subtract the average memory usage of the zygote that forked the process (its parent). This provides a better estimate of the child process's unique memory usage by accounting for the baseline memory inherited from its zygote. This is a no-op for a process whose forking zygote has no memory samples in the trace (e.g. traces with only ftrace rss_stat, where idle zygotes are not sampled; process_stats polling captures them). NOTE: Some tracks may have a spike greater than 100MiB. This can be legitimate or an accounting issue: see b/418231246 for more details.

templates.name: memory_oom_score_with_rss_and_swap_per_process
templates.description: Process memory and it's OOM adjuster scores. Detects transitions, each new interval means that either the memory or OOM adjuster score of the process changed.

templates.name: android_monitor_contention
templates.description: Contains parsed Java monitor contention slices, detailing the blocking and blocked threads, methods involved, and the duration of the contention.

templates.name: _children
templates.description: Monitor contention slices that are blocked by another monitor contention slice. They will have a |parent_id| field which is the id of the slice they are blocked by.

templates.name: _parents
templates.description: Monitor contention slices that are blocking another monitor contention slice. They will have a |child_id| field which is the id of the slice they are blocking.

templates.name: _isolated
templates.description: Monitor contention slices that are neither blocking nor blocked by another monitor contention slice. They neither have |parent_id| nor |child_id| fields.

templates.name: android_monitor_contention_chain
templates.description: Contains parsed monitor contention slices with the parent-child relationships.

templates.name: _first_blocked_contention
templates.description: First blocked node on a lock, i.e nodes with |waiter_count| = 0. The |dur| here is adjusted to only account for the time between the first thread waiting and the first thread to acquire the lock. That way, the thread state span joins below only compute the thread states where the blocking thread is actually holding the lock. This avoids counting the time when another waiter acquired the lock before the first waiter.

templates.name: android_monitor_contention_chain_thread_state
templates.description: Contains the span join of the first waiters in the |android_monitor_contention_chain| with their blocking_thread thread state. Note that we only span join the duration where the lock was actually held and contended. This can be less than the duration the lock was 'waited on' when a different waiter acquired the lock earlier than the first waiter.

templates.name: android_monitor_contention_chain_thread_state_by_txn
templates.description: Aggregated thread_states on the 'blocking thread', the thread holding the lock. This builds on the data from |android_monitor_contention_chain| and for each contention slice, it returns the aggregated sum of all the thread states on the blocking thread. Note that this data is only available for the first waiter on a lock.

templates.name: android_monitor_contention_chain_blocked_functions_by_txn
templates.description: Aggregated blocked_functions on the 'blocking thread', the thread holding the lock. This builds on the data from |android_monitor_contention_chain| and for each contention, it returns the aggregated sum of all the kernel blocked function durations on the blocking thread. Note that this data is only available for the first waiter on a lock.

templates.name: android_monitor_contention_graph
templates.description: Returns a DAG of all Java lock contentions in a process. Each node in the graph is a <thread:Java method> pair. Each edge connects from a node waiting on a lock to a node holding a lock. The weights of each node represent the cumulative wall time the node blocked other nodes connected to it.

templates.name: android_network_packets
templates.description: Android network packet events (from android.network_packets data source).

templates.name: _add_overlap_group_id
templates.description: Finds groups of overlapping slices and assigns them group ids. An overlap group is a set of slices (or instants) that contiguously have >=1 slice present. For example, the following is one group: ###   ## ##### Overlap detection is partitioned by the partition columns, where overlaps are only considered within the same partition. Returns $src with two additional columns: * group_id: the group the row belongs to (per partition_columns) * max_end_so_far: the maximum end timestamp observed so far, useful for determining the time since the last group or event (per partition_columns)

templates.name: android_network_uptime_spans
templates.description: Computes network uptime spans based on an idle timeout model. It is common in networking to have an interface active for some time after use. For example, mobile networks are often connected for 10 or more seconds after the last packet is sent or received. This macro simulates this timeout and returns spans that approximate the underlying connected regions.

templates.name: android_network_uptime_cost
templates.description: Compute the per-row uptime cost of network activity. It is common in networking to have an interface active for some time after use. For example, mobile networks are often connected for 10 or more seconds after the last packet is sent or received. This macro computes a cost factor indicating how much each row impacts the idle timer. For example, assuming a 10s timeout, the first packet will extend the timeout 10s in the future, and be assigned 10s of cost. If a packet arrives 4s later, it pushes the timer an additional 4s, receiving 4s of cost. In this simple case, cost is MIN(ts-last_packet_ts, timeout). The complication is that network events can be aggregates, with more than one packet. In such cases, we end up with a span with non-zero duration, rather than an instant, and no easy way to compute time since the last packet. The solution is to detect overlap regions and compute cost for the region as a whole. The first event in each group receives the standard uptime cost as described above. Each group has an additional cost equal to the duration of the group which is distributed using packet count as weight. For example (times in seconds, no partition, and 10 second timeout): ``` ts=5,  dur=0, packet_count=1  -> group=1, uptime_cost=10 ts=7,  dur=0, packet_count=1  -> group=2, uptime_cost=2 ts=20, dur=5, packet_count=9  -> group=3, uptime_cost=14.5 ts=22, dur=0, packet_count=1  -> group=3, uptime_cost=0.5 ``` The third group spans ts=20 to ts=25, with a timeout at ts=35. This gives the group a total cost of 15 which is distributed between the two rows. The 3rd row receives 10s for being first, and 9/10 the duration cost (5*9/10=4.5). The returned table schema is (id ID, uptime_cost INT64) where uptime cost is in nanoseconds.

templates.name: android_oom_adj_score_to_bucket_name

templates.name: android_oom_adj_score_to_detailed_bucket_name

templates.name: android_is_kernel_task

templates.name: android_process_metadata

templates.name: android_profiling_manager_cold_startup
templates.description: Provides cold startups for redacted traces Note: Redacted traces contain a subset of slices compare to a regular perfetto trace as they contain information only about the process starting its own profiling, thus, queries in this section may omit process information as its assumed that slices belong to the single profiled process.

templates.name: _android_generate_start_to_end_slices
templates.description: Returns an artificially generated slice for each pair of (matched(startSlicePattern), matched(endSlicePattern)) Caveat: If multiple slices match endSlicePattern it will only return one per start slice and it will be closest slice in the timeline.

templates.name: android_screen_state
templates.description: Table of the screen state - on, off or doze (always on display).

templates.name: _bind_dispatch

templates.name: _bind_receive
templates.description: Details of all Service#onBind received events.

templates.name: android_service_bindings
templates.description: All service bindings from client app to server app.

templates.name: android_standardize_slice_name
templates.description: Some slice names have params in them. This functions removes them to make it possible to aggregate by name. Some examples are: - Lock/monitor contention slices. The name includes where the lock contention is in the code. That part is removed. - DrawFrames/ooFrame. The name also includes the frame number. - Apk/oat/dex loading: The name of the apk is removed

templates.name: _normalize_android_string
templates.description: Maps slice names with common prefixes to a static string key. Returns NULL if there's no mapping.

templates.name: _startup_breakdown_reason
templates.description: Derives a startup reason from a slice name and some thread_state columns.

templates.name: _startup_root_slices
templates.description: List of startups with unique ids for each possible upid. The existing startup_ids are not necessarily unique (because of multiuser).

templates.name: _startup_normalized_slices
templates.description: All relevant startup slices normalized with _normalize_android_string.

templates.name: _startup_slices_breakdown
templates.description: Subset of _startup_normalized_slices that occurred during any app startups on the main thread. Their timestamps and durations are chopped to fit within the respective app startup duration.

templates.name: _startup_flat_slices_breakdown
templates.description: Flattened slice version of _startup_slices_breakdown. This selects the leaf slice at every region of the slice stack.

templates.name: _startup_thread_states_breakdown
templates.description: Subset of thread_states that occurred during any app startups on the main thread.

templates.name: android_startup_opinionated_breakdown
templates.description: Blended thread state and slice breakdown blocking app startups. Each row blames a unique period during an app startup with a reason derived from the slices and thread states on the main thread. Some helpful events to enables are binder transactions, ART, am and view.

templates.name: _mipmap_startup
templates.description: Create a table with unique startup events, including thread and process information.

templates.name: _mipmap_flat_slice
templates.description: Flatten slices that occur within the startups.

templates.name: _mipmap_flat_slice_thread_states_and_slices
templates.description: Create a table from the span join results.

templates.name: _mipmap_startup_slice
templates.description: Intersect the slices with thread states with the unique startup intervals. This table contains the slices that occurred during each startup.

templates.name: _mipmap_startup_buckets_1ms
templates.description: Creates 1ms buckets for startup intervals. This table uses the `_mipmap_buckets_table` macro to generate a series of 1-ms buckets for each startup. These buckets will be used to aggregate and summarize the startup activity.

templates.name: _mipmap_startup_slices_with_ids
templates.description: Prepares startup slices for mipmapping. This table assigns a unique ID and a `group_hash` to each startup slice. The `group_hash` is created from various properties of the slice, such as its name, depth, and thread state. Slices with the same `group_hash` are considered similar and can be merged during the mipmapping process.

templates.name: _android_startup_mipmap_1ms
templates.description: Creates a 1ms resolution mipmap of Android startup slices. This table uses the `_mipmap_merged` macro to generate a mipmap of the startup slices. The mipmap provides a summarized view of the startup, with a resolution of 1 ms. The table contains merged slices representing the dominant event in each time bucket.

templates.name: _startup_indicator_slices
templates.description: Create a table containing only the slices which are necessary for determining whether a startup happened.

templates.name: android_startup_processes
templates.description: Maps a startup to the set of processes that handled the activity start. The vast majority of cases should be a single process. However it is possible that the process dies during the activity startup and is respawned.

templates.name: android_startups
templates.description: All activity startups in the trace by startup id. Populated by different scripts depending on the platform version/contents.

templates.name: android_startup_threads
templates.description: Maps a startup to the set of threads on processes that handled the activity start.

templates.name: android_thread_slices_for_all_startups
templates.description: All the slices for all startups in trace. Generally, this view should not be used. Instead, use one of the view functions related to the startup slices which are created from this table.

templates.name: android_slices_for_startup_and_slice_name
templates.description: Given a startup id and GLOB for a slice name, returns matching slices with data.

templates.name: android_class_loading_for_startup
templates.description: A Perfetto view that lists matching slices for class loading during app startup.

templates.name: android_binder_transaction_slices_for_startup
templates.description: Returns binder transaction slices for a given startup id with duration over threshold.

templates.name: android_sum_dur_for_startup_and_slice
templates.description: Returns duration of startup for slice name. Sums duration of all slices of startup with provided name.

templates.name: android_sum_dur_on_main_thread_for_startup_and_slice
templates.description: Returns duration of startup for slice name on main thread. Sums duration of all slices of startup with provided name only on main thread.

templates.name: _activity_intent_received
templates.description: Marks the beginning of the trace and is equivalent to when the statsd startup logging begins.

templates.name: _activity_intent_recv_spans
templates.description: We partition the trace into spans based on posted activity intents. We will refine these progressively in the next steps to only encompass activity starts.

templates.name: _startup_partitions
templates.description: Filter activity_intent_recv_spans, keeping only the ones that triggered a startup.

templates.name: _activity_intent_startup_successful
templates.description: Successful activity startup. The end of the 'launching' event is not related to whether it actually succeeded or not.

templates.name: _startups_minsdk29
templates.description: Use the starting event package name. The finish event package name is not reliable in the case of failed startups.

templates.name: _ttid
templates.description: Get Time To Initial Display of the startup calculated as time between the startup started and the first frame that was started by Choreographer on the UI thread of the startup finished drawing. TTID (https://developer.android.com/topic/performance/vitals/launch-time#time-initial) Googlers: see go/android-performance-metrics-glossary for details.

templates.name: _ttfd
templates.description: Get Time To Full Display of the startup calculated as time between the startup started and the first frame that was started by Choreographer after or containing the `reportFullyDrawn()` slice on the UI thread of the startup finished drawing. TTFD (https://developer.android.com/topic/performance/vitals/launch-time#retrieve-TTFD) Googlers: see go/android-performance-metrics-glossary for details.

templates.name: android_startup_time_to_display
templates.description: Startup metric defintions, which focus on the observable time range: TTID - Time To Initial Display * https://developer.android.com/topic/performance/vitals/launch-time#time-initial * end of first RenderThread.DrawFrame - bindApplication TTFD - Time To Full Display * https://developer.android.com/topic/performance/vitals/launch-time#retrieve-TTFD * end of next RT.DrawFrame, after reportFullyDrawn called - bindApplication Googlers: see go/android-performance-metrics-glossary for details.

templates.name: android_statsd_atoms
templates.description: Statsd atoms. A subset of the slice table containing statsd atom instant events.

templates.name: _android_statsd_perfetto_triggers
templates.description: Information about Perfetto triggers, extracted from statsd atoms, which happened during the trace. This requires the `android.statsd` data-source to be enabled and the `ATOM_PERFETTO_TRIGGER` push atom to be configured.

templates.name: _android_sf_thread
templates.description: Extract thread and track information for a given thread in the surfaceflinger process.

templates.name: android_app_to_sf_frame_timeline_match
templates.description: Match the frame timeline on the app side with the frame timeline on the SF side. In cases where there are multiple layers drawn, there would be separate frame timeline slice for each of the layers. GROUP BY is used to deduplicate these rows.

templates.name: _android_jank_cuj_app_sf_frame_timeline_match
templates.description: Extract app and SF frame vsync scoped to CUJs.

templates.name: _find_android_jank_cuj_sf_main_thread_slice
templates.description: Extract ts and dur for a given slice name from the SF process main thread track.

templates.name: _android_jank_cuj_sf_main_thread_frame_boundary
templates.description: Calculates the frame boundaries based on when we *expected* the work to start and we use the end of the `composite` slice as the end of the work on the frame.

templates.name: android_surfaceflinger_workloads
templates.description: Workloads that are submitted to SurfaceFlinger to do compositing work

templates.name: android_suspend_state
templates.description: Table of suspended and awake slices. Selects either the minimal or full ftrace source depending on what's available, marks suspended periods, and complements them to give awake periods.

templates.name: _extract_duration_without_suspend
templates.description: Extracts the duration without counting CPU suspended time from an event. This is the same as converting an event duration from wall clock to monotonic clock. If there was no CPU suspend, the result is same as |dur|.

templates.name: android_standardize_thread_name
templates.description: Standardizes an Android thread name by extracting its core identifier to make it possible to aggregate by name. Removes extra parts of a thread name, like identifiers, leaving only the main prefix. Splits the name at ('-', '[', ':' , ' '). Some Examples: Given thread_name = "RenderThread-1[123]", returns "RenderThread". Given thread_name = "binder:5543_E" returns "binder". Given thread_name = "pool-3-thread-5", returns "pool". Given thread_name = "MainThread", returns "MainThread".

NOTE: this view of top level scrolls is based on the LatencyInfo definition of a scroll, which differs subtly from the definition based on EventLatencies.
