templates.name: chrome_scroll_update_info
templates.description: Timestamps and durations for the critical path stages during scrolling. This table covers both the input-associated (before coalescing inputs into a frame) and frame-associated (after coalescing inputs into a frame) stages of a scroll: ... | +--------------+--------------+ |                             | V                             V +-------------------------+   +-------------------------+ | _scroll_update_INPUT_   |   | _scroll_update_FRAME_   | | timestamps_and_metadata |   | timestamps_and_metadata | +------------+------------+   +------------+------------+ |                             | V                             V +-----------------------+     +-----------------------+ | chrome_scroll_update_ |     | chrome_scroll_update_ | |     INPUT_pipeline    |     |     FRAME_pipeline    | +-----------+-----------+     +-----------+-----------+ |                             | +--------------+--------------+ | V +---------------------------+ | chrome_scroll_update_info | +---------------------------+

templates.name: _chrome_scroll_frame_stage_delta
templates.description: Helper macro to compute the stage delta. Should be used only as a part of `chrome_scroll_frame_info`.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_frame_info
templates.description: A list of all presented Chrome frames which contain scroll updates and associated metadata.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_update_info_step_templates
templates.description: Source of truth for the definition of the stages of a scroll. Mainly intended for visualization purposes (e.g. in Chrome Scroll Jank plugin).

templates.name: _if_real_first_scroll_update
templates.description: A helper macro which evaluates to `expression` if the first scroll update in a frame is real. Otherwise, evaluates to NULL. Should be only used in `chrome_scroll_frame_info_v4`.

templates.name: _if_damaging_frame
templates.description: A helper macro which evaluates to `expression` if a frame is damaging. Otherwise, evaluates to NULL. Should be only used in `chrome_scroll_frame_info_v4`.

templates.name: _stage_dur_delta_v4
templates.description: Helper macro to compute the delta between the duration of this frame's and previous frame's `stage`. Should be only used in `chrome_scroll_frame_info_v4`.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_frame_info_v4
templates.description: A list of all presented Chrome frames which contain scroll updates together with additional metadata, pipeline stages breakdown and jank classification according to the scroll model of Chrome's scroll jank v4 metric.

templates.name: _descendant_slice_begin
templates.description: Finds the start timestamp for a given slice's descendant with a given name. If there are multiple descendants with a given name, the function will return the first one, so it's most useful when working with a timeline broken down into phases, where each subphase can happen only once.

templates.name: _descendant_slice_end
templates.description: Finds the end timestamp for a given slice's descendant with a given name. If there are multiple descendants with a given name, the function will return the first one, so it's most useful when working with a timeline broken down into phases, where each subphase can happen only once.

templates.name: _has_descendant_slice_with_name
templates.description: Checks if slice has a descendant with provided name.

templates.name: _get_presentation_timestamp
templates.description: Returns the presentation timestamp for a given EventLatency slice. This is either the end of SwapEndToPresentationCompositorFrame (if it exists), the end of LatchToPresentation (if it exists), the end of SwapStartToPresentation (if it exists), or the end of LatchToSwapEnd (workaround in older Chrome versions).

templates.name: chrome_gesture_scroll_updates
templates.description: All scroll-related events (frames) including gesture scroll updates, begins and ends with respective scroll ids and start/end timestamps, regardless of being presented. This includes pinches that were presented. See b/315761896 for context on pinches.

templates.name: chrome_event_latency_stage_descriptions
templates.description: Source of truth of the descriptions of EventLatency stages.

templates.name: chrome_graphics_pipeline_surface_frame_steps
templates.description: `Graphics.Pipeline` steps corresponding to work done by a Viz client to produce a frame (i.e. before surface aggregation). Covers steps: * STEP_ISSUE_BEGIN_FRAME * STEP_RECEIVE_BEGIN_FRAME * STEP_GENERATE_RENDER_PASS * STEP_GENERATE_COMPOSITOR_FRAME * STEP_SUBMIT_COMPOSITOR_FRAME * STEP_RECEIVE_COMPOSITOR_FRAME * STEP_RECEIVE_BEGIN_FRAME_DISCARD * STEP_DID_NOT_PRODUCE_FRAME * STEP_DID_NOT_PRODUCE_COMPOSITOR_FRAME

templates.name: chrome_graphics_pipeline_display_frame_steps
templates.description: `Graphics.Pipeline` steps corresponding to work done on creating and presenting one frame during/after surface aggregation. Covers steps: * STEP_DRAW_AND_SWAP * STEP_SURFACE_AGGREGATION * STEP_SEND_BUFFER_SWAP * STEP_BUFFER_SWAP_POST_SUBMIT * STEP_FINISH_BUFFER_SWAP * STEP_SWAP_BUFFERS_ACK

templates.name: chrome_surface_frame_id_to_first_display_id
templates.description: Links surface frames (`chrome_graphics_pipeline_surface_frame_steps`) to the the first display frame (`chrome_graphics_pipeline_display_frame_steps`) into which it was included. As an display frame usually aggregates frames from multiple surfaces, multiple `surface_frame_trace_id`s will correspond to one `display_trace_id`.

templates.name: chrome_graphics_pipeline_inputs_to_surface_frames
templates.description: Links inputs (`chrome_input_pipeline_steps.latency_id`) to the surface frame (`chrome_graphics_pipeline_surface_frame_steps`) to which they correspond. In other words, in general, multiple `latency_id`s will correspond to one `surface_frame_trace_id`.

templates.name: chrome_histograms
templates.description: A helper view on top of the histogram events emitted by Chrome. Requires "disabled-by-default-histogram_samples" Chrome category or the "org.chromium.histogram_sample" data source.

templates.name: _chrome_input_pipeline_steps_no_input_type
templates.description: Processing steps of the Chrome input pipeline.

templates.name: chrome_inputs
templates.description: Each row represents one input pipeline.

templates.name: chrome_input_pipeline_steps
templates.description: Since not all steps have associated input type (but all steps for a given latency id should have the same input type), populate input type for steps where it would be NULL.

templates.name: chrome_coalesced_inputs
templates.description: For each input, if it was coalesced into another input, get the other input's latency id.

templates.name: chrome_touch_move_to_scroll_update
templates.description: Each scroll update event (except flings) in Chrome starts its life as a touch move event, which is then eventually converted to a scroll update itself. Each of these events is represented by its own LatencyInfo. This table contains a mapping between touch move events and scroll update events they were converted into.

templates.name: chrome_dispatch_android_input_event_to_touch_move
templates.description: Matches Android input id to the corresponding touch move event.

templates.name: chrome_interactions
templates.description: All critical user interaction events, including type and table with associated metrics.

templates.name: _chrome_jetstream_3_benchmark_name
templates.description: Mapping of JetStream 3 leaf benchmarks to their top-level groups and worst-case counts. Each top-level group has its score calculated separately, before all group scores are combined. Null top-level groups become ungrouped benchmarks.

templates.name: chrome_jetstream_3_renderer_main_utid
templates.description: Find the main thread of the JetStream 3 renderer.

templates.name: chrome_jetstream_3_measure
templates.description: Final list of JetStream 3 measures. Classified into: * 'First' (Iteration 0), * 'Average' (Iteration 1..N) * 'Worst' (slowest iterations). Note: 'Average' includes all iterations except the first and the worst ones. In the actual benchmark scoring, 'Average' includes 'Worst', but the measure table avoids listing the same slice twice. For WSL, the subtest name is the slice name (e.g., 'WSL-stdlib').

templates.name: _chrome_jetstream_3_subtest_score
templates.description: Calculate score for each subtest classification. A subtest score is 5000 / arithmetic mean of durations in milliseconds.

templates.name: _chrome_jetstream_3_leaf_benchmark_score
templates.description: Calculate the score for each leaf benchmark. A benchmark's score is the geometric mean of its subtest scores (First, Average, Worst). Note: some benchmarks might not have exactly these three subtests, e.g. WSL.

templates.name: chrome_jetstream_3_benchmark_score
templates.description: Calculate the score for each top-level benchmark. - If a benchmark is grouped, its score is the geometric mean of its children's scores. Otherwise, for ungrouped benchmarks, their score is the top-level score.

templates.name: chrome_jetstream_3_score
templates.description: Overall JetStream 3 score. The final score is the geometric mean of all top-level benchmark scores.

templates.name: _chrome_get_next_presentation_time_by_pid
templates.description: Gets the presentation time of the first frame committed after ts in the renderer process with pid.

templates.name: _chrome_loadline2_marks_with_pid
templates.description: User timing trace events can be emitted by either performance.mark() or performance.measure(). The former appear on the CrRendererMain thread track, the latter on their own custom track inside the Renderer process. This query looks for the event track info in both thread_track and process_track to support both cases.

templates.name: _chrome_loadline2_story_pid
templates.description: Renderer process pid for each page

templates.name: _chrome_loadline2_story_start_with_pid
templates.description: Story start and Renderer pid for each page

templates.name: _chrome_loadline2_start_request
templates.description: Start timestamp for the first network request for each page

templates.name: _chrome_loadline2_end_request
templates.description: Finish timestamp for the first network request for each page

templates.name: _chrome_loadline2_visual_raf
templates.description: Timestamp of the second rAF after visual mark for each page

templates.name: _chrome_loadline2_interactive_raf
templates.description: Timestamp of the second rAF after interactive mark for each page

templates.name: chrome_hardware_class
templates.description: Returns hardware class of the device, often use to find device brand and model.

templates.name: chrome_page_loads
templates.description: Chrome page loads, including associated high-level metrics and properties.

templates.name: _get_slow_scroll_delta_threshold
templates.description: The maximum delta value between three consecutive frames, used to determine whether the sequence in the scroll is fast or slow; the sequence speed is used to determine whether the sequence includes any predictor jank.

templates.name: _get_slow_scroll_janky_threshold
templates.description: The threshold for the ratio of the delta of middle frame to tbe deltas of its neighbors in a sequence of three frames, if the sequence is considered "slow".

templates.name: _get_fast_scroll_janky_threshold
templates.description: The threshold for the ratio of the delta of middle frame to tbe deltas of its neighbors in a sequence of three frames, if the sequence is considered "fast".

templates.name: _get_scroll_jank_threshold
templates.description: Determine the acceptable threshold (see _get_slow_scroll_janky_threshold() and _get_fast_scroll_janky_threshold()) based on the maximum delta value between three consecutive frames.

templates.name: _get_predictor_jank
templates.description: Calculate the predictor jank of three consecutive frames, if it is above the threshold. Anything below the threshold is not considered jank.

templates.name: chrome_predictor_error
templates.description: The scrolling offsets and predictor jank values for the actual (applied) scroll events.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_jank_cause_descriptions
templates.description: Source of truth of the descriptions of EventLatency-based scroll jank causes.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_jank_causes_with_event_latencies
templates.description: Combined description of scroll jank cause and associated event latency stage.

templates.name: _get_process_id_for_surfaceflinger
templates.description: Function to retrieve the upid for a surfaceflinger, as these are attributed to the GPU but are recorded on a different data source (and track group).

templates.name: _process_type_to_name
templates.description: Map a generic process type to a specific name or substring of a name that can be found in the trace process table.

templates.name: _get_process_id_by_type
templates.description: TODO(b/309937901): Add chrome instance id for multiple chromes/webviews in a trace, as this may result in  multiple browser and GPU processes. Function to retrieve the chrome process ID for a specific process type. Does not retrieve the Renderer process, as this is determined when the EventLatency is known. See function _get_renderer_upid_for_event_latency below.

templates.name: _get_renderer_upid_for_event_latency
templates.description: Function to retrieve the chrome process ID that a given EventLatency slice occurred on. This is the Renderer process.

templates.name: _processes_by_type_for_event_latency
templates.description: Helper function to retrieve all of the upids for a given process, thread, or EventLatency.

templates.name: chrome_select_scroll_jank_cause_thread
templates.description: Function to retrieve the thread id of the thread on a particular process if there are any slices during a particular EventLatency slice duration; this upid/thread combination refers to a cause of Scroll Jank.

templates.name: chrome_janky_event_latencies_v3
templates.description: Selects EventLatency slices that correspond with janks in a scroll. This is based on the V3 version of scroll jank metrics.

templates.name: chrome_janky_frame_presentation_intervals
templates.description: Frame presentation interval is the delta between when the frame was supposed to be presented and when it was actually presented.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_stats
templates.description: Scroll jank frame presentation stats for individual scrolls.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_jank_intervals_v3
templates.description: Defines slices for all of janky scrolling intervals in a trace.

templates.name: _slice_name_from_id
templates.description: Given a slice id, returns the name of the slice.

templates.name: chrome_presented_gesture_scrolls
templates.description: Scroll updates, corresponding to all input events that were converted to a presented scroll update.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_updates_with_deltas
templates.description: Associate every trace_id with it's perceived delta_y on the screen after prediction.

templates.name: chrome_full_frame_view
templates.description: Obtain the subset of input events that were fully presented.

templates.name: chrome_merged_frame_view
templates.description: Group all gestures presented at the same timestamp together in a single row.

templates.name: chrome_frame_info_with_delay
templates.description: View contains all chrome presented frames during gesture updates while calculating delay since last presented which usually should equal to |VSYNC_INTERVAL| if no jank is present.

templates.name: chrome_vsyncs
templates.description: Calculate |VSYNC_INTERVAL| as the lowest vsync seen in the trace or the minimum delay between frames larger than zero. TODO(~M130): Remove the lowest vsync since we should always have vsync_interval_ms.

templates.name: chrome_janky_frames_no_cause
templates.description: Filter the frame view only to frames that had missed vsyncs.

templates.name: chrome_janky_frames_no_subcause
templates.description: Janky frame information including the jank cause.

templates.name: chrome_janky_frames
templates.description: Finds all causes of jank for all janky frames, and a cause of sub jank if the cause of jank was GPU related.

templates.name: chrome_unique_frame_presentation_ts
templates.description: Counting all unique frame presentation timestamps.

templates.name: chrome_janky_frames_percentage
templates.description: Dividing missed frames over total frames to get janky frame percentage. This represents the v3 scroll jank metrics. Reflects Event.Jank.DelayedFramesPercentage UMA metric.

templates.name: chrome_frames_per_scroll
templates.description: Number of frames and janky frames per scroll.

templates.name: _direct_children_slice
templates.description: Finds all slices with a direct parent with the given parent_id.

templates.name: chrome_get_v3_jank_cause_id
templates.description: Given two slice Ids A and B, find the maximum difference between the durations of it's direct children with matching names for example if slice A has children named (X, Y, Z) with durations of (10, 10, 5) and slice B has children named (X, Y) with durations of (9, 9), the function will return the slice id of the slice named Z that is A's child, as no matching slice named Z was found under B, making 5 - 0 = 5 the maximum delta between both slice's direct children

templates.name: chrome_scroll_input_deltas
templates.description: The raw input deltas for all input events which were part of a scroll.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_input_offsets
templates.description: The raw coordinates and pixel offsets for all input events which were part of a scroll.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_presented_deltas
templates.description: The page offset delta (by how much the page was scrolled vs previous frame) for each frame. This is the resulting delta that is shown to the user after the input has been processed. `chrome_scroll_input_deltas` tracks the underlying signal deltas between consecutive input events.

templates.name: chrome_presented_scroll_offsets
templates.description: The scrolling offsets for the actual (applied) scroll events. These are not necessarily inclusive of all user scroll events, rather those scroll events that are actually processed.

templates.name: _is_janky_frame
templates.description: Function : function takes scroll ids of frames to verify it's from the same scroll, and makes sure the frame ts occurred within the scroll timestamp of the neighbour and computes whether the frame was janky or not.

templates.name: _jank_budget
templates.description: Function : function takes the cur_frame_exact, prev_frame_exact and next_frame_exact and returns the value of the jank budget of the current frame. jank_budget is the minimum amount of frames/time we need to reduce the frame duration by for it to be no longer considered janky. Returns the jank budget in percentage (i.e. 0.75) of vsync interval percentage.

templates.name: chrome_select_long_task_slices
templates.description: Extract mojo information for the long-task-tracking scenario for specific names. For example, LongTaskTracker slices may have associated IPC metadata, or InterestingTask slices for input may have associated IPC to determine whether the task is fling/etc.

templates.name: _prepare_janky_scroll_frames
templates.description: A helper macro to avoid manually filtering `scroll_frames` multiple times. Returns janky rows of `scroll_frames` with a few additional statistics.

templates.name: _chrome_janky_scroll_frames
templates.description: List of janky scroll frames according to Chrome's v1 scroll jank metric.

templates.name: _chrome_janky_scroll_frames_v4
templates.description: List of janky scroll frames according to Chrome's v4 scroll jank metric.

templates.name: _chrome_scroll_jank_tag_long_stage
templates.description: A helper macro to generate tags for long stages in the scroll pipeline.

templates.name: _assign_scroll_jank_tags_to_frames
templates.description: A helper macro which assigns scroll jank causes to janky scroll frames. The result contains zero or more tags for each janky frame.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_jank_tags
templates.description: List of scroll jank causes that apply to scroll frames which are janky according to Chrome's v1 scroll jank metric. Contains zero or more tags for each janky frame.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_jank_tags_v4
templates.description: List of scroll jank causes that apply to scroll frames which are janky according to Chrome's v4 scroll jank metric. Contains zero or more tags for each janky frame.

templates.name: _consolidate_tagged_janky_scroll_frames
templates.description: A helper macro which flattens the tags for each janky scroll frame. The result contains exactly one row for each frame in `janky_scroll_frames`.

templates.name: chrome_tagged_janky_scroll_frames
templates.description: Scroll frames which are janky according to Chrome's v1 scroll jank metric together with the assigned causes. Contains exactly one row for each janky frame in `chrome_scroll_frame_info`.

templates.name: chrome_tagged_janky_scroll_frames_v4
templates.description: Scroll frames which are janky according to Chrome's v4 scroll jank metric together with the assigned causes. Contains exactly one row for each janky frame in `chrome_scroll_frame_info_v4`.

templates.name: _chrome_scroll_jank_v4_sub_slice
templates.description: Finds the timestamps and durations of sub-slices of 'ScrollJankV4' main slices with the given `sub_slice_names`. If available (in newer Chrome versions), we associate sub-slices with the main slice based on the `scroll_jank_v4.result_id` argument. Otherwise (in older Chrome versions), we associate sub-slices with the main slice based on the `descendant_slice` operator instead. We prefer the former approach because it's more robust. See below for more details. The returned table is guaranteed to contain at most one row for each 'ScrollJankV4' slice ID in `scroll_jank_v4_slice`.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_jank_v4_results
templates.description: Results of the Scroll Jank V4 metric for frames which contain one or more scroll updates. See https://docs.google.com/document/d/1AaBvTIf8i-c-WTKkjaL4vyhQMkSdynxo3XEiwpofdeA and `EventLatency.ScrollJankV4Result` in https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:base/tracing/protos/chrome_track_event.proto for more information. Available since Chrome 145.0.7573.0 and cherry-picked into 144.0.7559.31.

templates.name: chrome_scroll_jank_v4_reasons
templates.description: Reasons why the Scroll Jank V4 metric marked frames as janky. A frame might be janky for multiple reasons, so this table might contain multiple rows with the same `id` and distinct `jank_reason`s. Available since Chrome 145.0.7573.0 and cherry-picked into 144.0.7559.31.

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_measure
templates.description: Augmented slices for Speedometer measurements. These are the intervals of time Speedometer uses to compute the final score. There are two intervals that are measured for every test: sync and async

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_iteration
templates.description: Slice that covers one Speedometer iteration. Depending on the Speedometer version these slices might need to be estimated as older versions of Speedometer to not emit marks for this interval. The metrics associated are the same ones Speedometer would output, but note we use ns precision (Speedometer uses ~100us) so the actual values might differ a bit.

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_score
templates.description: Returns the Speedometer score for all iterations in the trace

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_renderer_main_utid
templates.description: Returns the utid for the main thread that ran Speedometer 3

templates.name: _chrome_speedometer_2_1_mark_name
templates.description: List Speedometer 2.1 test marks. Used to find relevant slices.

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_2_1_measure
templates.description: Augmented slices for Speedometer measurements. These are the intervals of time Speedometer uses to compute the final score. There are two intervals that are measured for every test: sync and async sync is the time between the start and sync-end marks, async is the time between the sync-end and async-end marks.

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_2_1_iteration
templates.description: Slice that covers one Speedometer iteration. This slice is actually estimated as a default Speedometer run will not emit marks to cover this interval. The metrics associated are the same ones Speedometer would output, but note we use ns precision (Speedometer uses ~100us) so the actual values might differ a bit. Also note Speedometer returns the values in ms these here and in ns.

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_2_1_score
templates.description: Returns the Speedometer 2.1 score for all iterations in the trace

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_2_1_renderer_main_utid
templates.description: Returns the utid for the main thread that ran Speedometer 2.1

templates.name: _chrome_speedometer_3_measure_name
templates.description: List Speedometer 3 measures. Used to find relevant slices.

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_3_measure
templates.description: Augmented slices for Speedometer measurements. These are the intervals of time Speedometer uses to compute the final score. There are two intervals that are measured for every test: sync and async.

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_3_iteration
templates.description: Slice that covers one Speedometer iteration. The metrics associated are the same ones Speedometer would output, but note we use ns precision (Speedometer uses ~100us) so the actual values might differ a bit.

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_3_score
templates.description: Returns the Speedometer 3 score for all iterations in the trace

templates.name: chrome_speedometer_3_renderer_main_utid
templates.description: Returns the utid for the main thread that ran Speedometer 3

templates.name: _startup_start_events
templates.description: Access all startups, including those that don't lead to any visible content. If TimeToFirstVisibleContent is available, then this event will be the main event of the startup. Otherwise, the event for the start timestamp will be used.

templates.name: _launch_causes
templates.description: Chrome launch causes, not recorded at start time; use the activity id to join with the actual startup events.

templates.name: _has_parent_slice_with_name
templates.description: Checks if slice has an ancestor with provided name.

templates.name: _extract_mojo_ipc_hash
templates.description: Returns the mojo ipc hash for a given task, looking it up from the argument of descendant ScopedSetIpcHash slice. This is relevant only for the older Chrome traces, where mojo IPC hash was reported in a separate ScopedSetIpcHash slice.

templates.name: _extract_frame_type
templates.description: Returns the frame type (main frame vs subframe) for key navigation tasks which capture the associated RenderFrameHost in an argument.

templates.name: _human_readable_navigation_task_name
templates.description: Human-readable aliases for a few key navigation tasks.

templates.name: _format_scheduler_task_name
templates.description: Takes a task name and formats it correctly for scheduler tasks.

templates.name: _java_not_top_level_category
templates.description: Takes the category and determines whether it is "Java" only, as opposed to "toplevel,Java".

templates.name: _any_top_level_category
templates.description: Takes the category and determines whether is any valid toplevel category or combination of categories.

templates.name: _get_java_views_task_type
templates.description: Get task type for a given task kind.

templates.name: _chrome_mojo_slices
templates.description: All slices corresponding to receiving mojo messages. On the newer Chrome versions, it's just "Receive mojo message" and "Receive mojo reply" slices (or "Receive {mojo_message_name}" if built with `extended_tracing_enabled`. On legacy Chrome versions, other appropriate messages (like "Connector::DispatchMessage") are used. @column STRING interface_name    Name of the IPC interface. @column INT ipc_hash             Hash of a message name. @column STRING message_type      Either 'message' or 'reply'. @column INT id                   Slice id. Note: this might include messages received within a sync mojo call. TODO(altimin): This should use EXTEND_TABLE when it becomes available.

templates.name: _chrome_java_views
templates.description: This table contains a list of slices corresponding to the _representative_ Chrome Java view operations. These are the outermost Java view slices after filtering out generic framework views (like FitWindowsLinearLayout) and selecting the outermost slices from the remaining ones. @column id INT                       Slice id. @column ts INT                       Timestamp. @column dur INT                      Duration. @column name STRING                  Name of the view. @column is_software_screenshot BOOL  Whether this slice is a part of non-accelerated capture toolbar screenshot. @column is_hardware_screenshot BOOL  Whether this slice is a part of accelerated capture toolbar screenshot.

templates.name: chrome_java_views
templates.description: A list of slices corresponding to operations on interesting (non-generic) Chrome Java views. The view is considered interested if it's not a system (ContentFrameLayout) or generic library (CompositorViewHolder) views. TODO(altimin): Add "columns_from slice" annotation. TODO(altimin): convert this to EXTEND_TABLE when it becomes available.

templates.name: _chrome_choreographer_tasks
templates.description: A list of Choreographer tasks (Android frame generation) in Chrome.

templates.name: _get_posted_from
templates.description: Extract task's posted_from information from task's arguments.

templates.name: _select_begin_main_frame_java_slices
templates.description: Selects the BeginMainFrame slices (which as posted from ScheduledActionSendBeginMainFrame), used for root-level processing. In top-level/Java based slices, these will correspond to the ancestor of descendant slices; in long-task tracking, these tasks will be on a custom track and will need to be associated with children by timestamp and duration. Corresponds with the Choreographer root slices in chrome_choreographer_tasks below. Schema: @column is            The slice id. @column kind          The type of Java slice. @column ts            The timestamp of the slice. @column name          The name of the slice.

templates.name: _chrome_slices_with_java_views
templates.description: A list of Chrome tasks which were performing operations with Java views, together with the names of these views. @column id INT            Slice id. @column kind STRING       Type of the task. @column java_views STRING Concatenated names of Java views used by the task.

templates.name: _chrome_scheduler_tasks
templates.description: A list of tasks executed by Chrome scheduler.

templates.name: chrome_scheduler_tasks
templates.description: A list of tasks executed by Chrome scheduler.

templates.name: _get_descendant_mojo_slice_candidate
templates.description: Select the slice that might be the descendant mojo slice for the given task slice if it exists.

templates.name: _chrome_tasks
templates.description: A list of "Chrome tasks": top-level execution units (e.g. scheduler tasks / IPCs / system callbacks) run by Chrome. For a given thread, the tasks will not intersect. @column task_name STRING  Name for the given task. @column task_type STRING  Type of the task (e.g. "scheduler"). @column scheduling_delay LONG

templates.name: chrome_tasks
templates.description: A list of "Chrome tasks": top-level execution units (e.g. scheduler tasks / IPCs / system callbacks) run by Chrome. For a given thread, the slices corresponding to these tasks will not intersect.

templates.name: chrome_vsync_intervals
templates.description: A simple table that checks the time between VSync (this can be used to determine if we're refreshing at 90 FPS or 60 FPS). Note: In traces without the "Java" category there will be no VSync TraceEvents and this table will be empty.

templates.name: chrome_calculate_avg_vsync_interval
templates.description: Function: compute the average Vysnc interval of the gesture (hopefully this would be either 60 FPS for the whole gesture or 90 FPS but that isnt always the case) on the given time segment. If the trace doesnt contain the VSync TraceEvent we just fall back on assuming its 60 FPS (this is the 1.6e+7 in the COALESCE which corresponds to 16 ms or 60 FPS).

templates.name: chrome_web_content_interactions
templates.description: Chrome web content interactions (InteractionToFirstPaint), including associated high-level metrics and properties. Multiple events may occur for the same interaction; each row in this table represents the primary (longest) event for the interaction. Web content interactions are discrete, as opposed to sustained (e.g. scrolling); and only occur with the web content itself, as opposed to other parts of Chrome (e.g. omnibox). Interaction events include taps, clicks, keyboard input (typing), and drags.

templates.name: counter_leading_intervals
templates.description: For a given counter timeline (e.g. a single counter track), returns intervals of time where the counter has the same value. For every run of identical values, this macro will return a row for the first one, the last one, and a row merging all other ones. This to to facilitate construction of counters from delta_values. Intervals are computed in a "forward-looking" way. That is, if a counter changes value at some timestamp, it's assumed it *just* reached that value and it should continue to have that value until the next value change. The final value is assumed to hold until the very end of the trace. For example, suppose we have the following data: ``` ts=0, value=10, track_id=1 ts=0, value=10, track_id=2 ts=10, value=10, track_id=1 ts=10, value=20, track_id=2 ts=20, value=30, track_id=1 [end of trace at ts = 40] ``` Then this macro will generate the following intervals: ``` ts=0, dur=10, value=10, track_id=1 ts=10, dur=10, value=10, track_id=1 ts=20, dur=10, value=30, track_id=1 ts=0, dur=10, value=10, track_id=2 ts=10, dur=30, value=20, track_id=2 ```

templates.name: export_to_firefox_profile

templates.name: dominator_compatible_heap_graph
templates.description: Given a table containing a directed flow-graph and an entry node, computes the "dominator tree" for the graph. See [1] for an explanation of what a dominator tree is. [1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dominator_(graph_theory) Example usage on traces containing heap graphs: ```

templates.name: graph_dominator_tree
templates.description: Given a table containing a directed flow-graph and an entry node, computes the "dominator tree" for the graph. See [1] for an explanation of what a dominator tree is. [1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dominator_(graph_theory) Example usage on traces containing heap graphs: ``` CREATE PERFETTO VIEW dominator_compatible_heap_graph AS -- Extract the edges from the heap graph which correspond to references -- between objects. SELECT owner_id AS source_node_id, owned_id as dest_node_id FROM heap_graph_reference JOIN heap_graph_object owner on heap_graph_reference.owner_id = owner.id WHERE owned_id IS NOT NULL AND owner.reachable UNION ALL -- Since a Java heap graph is a "forest" structure, we need to add a dummy -- "root" node which connects all the roots of the forest into a single -- connected component. SELECT (SELECT max(id) + 1 FROM heap_graph_object) as source_node_id, id FROM heap_graph_object WHERE root_type IS NOT NULL; SELECT * FROM graph_dominator_tree!( dominator_compatible_heap_graph, (SELECT max(id) + 1 FROM heap_graph_object) ); ```

templates.name: _tree_reachable_ancestors_or_self
templates.description: Given a table containing the edges in a tree and a table of start_nodes, returns the ids of all the nodes reachable by walking up the tree from each of the start nodes.

templates.name: tree_structural_partition_by_group
templates.description: Partitions a tree into a forest of trees based on a given grouping key in a structure-preserving way. Specifically, for each tree in the output forest, all the nodes in that tree have the same ancestors and descendants as in the original tree *iff* that ancestor/descendent belonged to the same group. Example: Input id | parent_id | group_key ---|-----------|---------- 1  | NULL      | 1 2  | 1         | 1 3  | NULL      | 2 4  | NULL      | 2 5  | 2         | 1 6  | NULL      | 3 7  | 4         | 2 8  | 4         | 1 Or as a graph: ``` 1 (1) / 2 (1) /  \ 3 (2) 4 (2) /   \ 5 (1) 8 (1) /  \ 6 (3) 7 (2) ``` Possible output (order of rows is implementation-defined) id | parent_id | group_key ---|-----------|------- 1  | NULL      | 1 2  | 1         | 1 3  | NULL      | 2 4  | NULL      | 2 5  | 2         | 1 6  | NULL      | 3 7  | 4         | 2 8  | 2         | 1 Or as a forest: ``` 1 (1)       3 (2)      4 (2)        6 (3) |                      | 2 (1)                  7 (2) /   \ 5 (1) 8 (1) ```

templates.name: _graph_scan
templates.description: Performs a "scan" over the graph starting at `init_table` and using `graph_table` for edges to follow. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prefix_sum#Scan_higher_order_function for details of what a scan means.

templates.name: _graph_aggregating_scan
templates.description: Performs a "scan" over the graph starting at `init_table` and using `graph_table` for edges to follow, aggregating on each node wherever possible using `agg_query`. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prefix_sum#Scan_higher_order_function for details of what a scan means.

templates.name: graph_reachable_dfs
templates.description: Computes the "reachable" set of nodes in a directed graph from a given set of starting nodes by performing a depth-first search on the graph. The returned nodes are structured as a tree with parent-child relationships corresponding to the order in which nodes were encountered by the DFS. While this macro can be used directly by end users (hence being public), it is primarily intended as a lower-level building block upon which higher level functions/macros in the standard library can be built. Example usage on traces containing heap graphs: ``` -- Compute the reachable nodes from the first heap root. SELECT * FROM graph_reachable_dfs!( ( SELECT owner_id AS source_node_id, owned_id as dest_node_id FROM heap_graph_reference WHERE owned_id IS NOT NULL ), (SELECT id FROM heap_graph_object WHERE root_type IS NOT NULL) ); ```

templates.name: graph_reachable_bfs
templates.description: Computes the "reachable" set of nodes in a directed graph from a given starting node by performing a breadth-first search on the graph. The returned nodes are structured as a tree with parent-child relationships corresponding to the order in which nodes were encountered by the BFS. While this macro can be used directly by end users (hence being public), it is primarily intended as a lower-level building block upon which higher level functions/macros in the standard library can be built. Example usage on traces containing heap graphs: ``` -- Compute the reachable nodes from all heap roots. SELECT * FROM graph_reachable_bfs!( ( SELECT owner_id AS source_node_id, owned_id as dest_node_id FROM heap_graph_reference WHERE owned_id IS NOT NULL ), (SELECT id FROM heap_graph_object WHERE root_type IS NOT NULL) ); ```

templates.name: graph_next_sibling
templates.description: Computes the next sibling node in a directed graph. The next node under a parent node is determined by on the |sort_key|, which should be unique for every node under a parent. The order of the next sibling is undefined if the |sort_key| is not unique. Example usage: ``` -- Compute the next sibling: SELECT * FROM graph_next_sibling!( ( SELECT id AS node_id, parent_id AS node_parent_id, ts AS sort_key FROM slice ) ); ```

templates.name: graph_reachable_weight_bounded_dfs
templates.description: Computes the "reachable" set of nodes in a directed graph from a set of starting (root) nodes by performing a depth-first search from each root node on the graph. The search is bounded by the sum of edge weights on the path and the root node specifies the max weight (inclusive) allowed before stopping the search. The returned nodes are structured as a tree with parent-child relationships corresponding to the order in which nodes were encountered by the DFS. Each row also has the root node from which where the edge was encountered. While this macro can be used directly by end users (hence being public), it is primarily intended as a lower-level building block upon which higher level functions/macros in the standard library can be built. Example usage on traces with sched info: ``` -- Compute the reachable nodes from a sched wakeup chain INCLUDE PERFETTO MODULE sched.thread_executing_spans; SELECT * FROM graph_reachable_dfs_bounded !( ( SELECT id AS source_node_id, COALESCE(parent_id, id) AS dest_node_id, id - COALESCE(parent_id, id) AS edge_weight FROM _wakeup_chain ), ( SELECT id AS root_node_id, id - COALESCE(prev_id, id) AS root_target_weight FROM _wakeup_chain )); ```

templates.name: _interval_create
templates.description: Given a table of start timestamps and a table of end timestamps, creates intervals by matching each start with the next end timestamp strictly greater than it. Both input tables must have a column named `ts`. Uses an efficient O(n+m) two-pointer algorithm implemented in C++. Example: ``` SELECT * FROM _interval_create!( (SELECT ts FROM starts_table), (SELECT ts FROM ends_table) ) ```

templates.name: __ifg_null
templates.description: Helper for __intrinsic_token_apply to fill in NULLs for a ColumnNameList.

templates.name: _intervals_fill_gaps
templates.description: Fills in gaps within a table of slices, so data is present from trace_start to trace_end for each group of columns. Given a table of slices (`ts`, `dur`, `group_cols`, `data_cols`), this macro returns a table where every distinct `group_cols` has contiguous data from trace_start to trace_end. The `data_cols` are carried through from the source and filled with nulls when gaps are found. The input must not have overlaps within a set of `group_cols` Notes: * If grouping is not required, you may pass `(NULL)` for group_cols. * To guarantee a group is present, it is safe to `UNION` a row with the correct `group_cols` and all other columns as NULL. Example: ``` _intervals_fill_gaps!( (uid, package_name), (process_state), process_state_table ) ```

templates.name: _interval_rename_cols
templates.description: Helper macro to rename columns to standard names

templates.name: _interval_intersect_with_col_names
templates.description: Intersects two tables of intervals, allowing custom column names for id, ts, and dur. Each table can have different column names. Example: SELECT * FROM _interval_intersect_with_col_names!( table1, id1, ts1, dur1, table2, id2, ts2, dur2, (partition_col) )

templates.name: interval_self_intersect
templates.description: Given a list of intervals (ts, dur), this macro generates a list of interval end points as well as the intervals that intersect with those points. e.g. input (10, 20), (20, 25) 10      30 A |-------| B|----------| 20         45 would generate the output: ``` ts,dur,group_id,id,interval_ends_at_ts 10,10,1,A,0 20,10,2,A,0 20,10,2,B,0 30,15,3,A,1 30,15,3,B,0 45,0,4,B,1 ``` Runtime is O(n log n + m), where n is the number of intervals and m is the size of the output.

templates.name: _mipmap_bucket_metadata
templates.description: _mipmap_bucket_metadata: Calculates time range and bucket counts for each partition. This macro determines the start time, end time, and the number of buckets needed to cover the entire duration of events within each partition defined by _partition_column.

templates.name: _mipmap_buckets_table
templates.description: _mipmap_buckets_table: Generates a table of fixed-duration time buckets(intervals). Each partition (defined by _partition_column) will have its own set of buckets starting from its trace_min_ts. Buckets are assigned a globally unique id.

templates.name: _mipmap_merged
templates.description: _mipmap_merged: Merges slices based on dominant overlap within time buckets. This macro intersects source slices with the generated buckets, determines the dominant slice group (by group_hash) in each bucket, and then merges contiguous buckets that share the same dominant group.

templates.name: intervals_overlap_count
templates.description: Compute the distribution of the overlap of the given intervals over time. Each interval is a (ts, dur) pair and the overlap represented as a (ts, value) counter, with the value corresponding to the number of intervals that overlap the given timestamp and interval until the next timestamp.

templates.name: intervals_overlap_count_by_group
templates.description: Compute the distribution of the overlap of the given intervals over time from slices in a same group. Each interval is a (ts, dur, group) triple and the overlap represented as a (ts, value, group) counter, with the value corresponding to the number of intervals that belong to the same group and overlap the given timestamp and interval until the next timestamp.

templates.name: _intervals_overlap_in_table
templates.description: Returns whether |intervals| contains any overlapping intervals. Useful for checking if provided table/subquery can be used for intervals_intersect macro.

templates.name: _intervals_merge_root_and_children_by_intersection
templates.description: This is very similar to _intervals_merge_root_and_children but there is no explicit root_id shared between the root and the children. Instead an _interval_intersect is used to derive the root and child relationships.

templates.name: _intervals_flatten
templates.description: Partition and flatten a hierarchy of intervals into non-overlapping intervals where each resulting interval is the leaf in the hierarchy at any given time. The result also denotes the 'self-time' of each interval. Each interval is a (root_id, root_ts, root_dur, id, parent_id, ts, dur) and the overlap is represented as a (root_id, id, parent_id, ts, dur). Note that, children intervals must not be longer than any ancestor interval. See _intervals_merge_root_and_children that can be used to generate input to this macro from two different root and children tables.

templates.name: interval_merge_overlapping
templates.description: Merge intervals when they overlap to generate a minimum covering set of intervals with no overlap. The intervals are closed (contain both endpoints) and we consider two intervals overlapping (a) the intervals overlap or (b) if the end point of one interval is within epsilon of the start point of the other.

templates.name: interval_merge_overlapping_partitioned
templates.description: Merge overlapping intervals within each partition group to generate a minimum covering set of intervals with no overlap within each partition. For each partition, this macro merges overlapping intervals into non-overlapping intervals. The result contains intervals where at least one input interval is active. For example, with partition 'A': Input: (ts=1, dur=10), (ts=5, dur=12) Output: (ts=1, dur=16)

templates.name: linux_active_block_io_operations_by_device
templates.description: View tracking the number of IO operations remaining in the kernel IO queue or a block device

templates.name: linux_device_major_id
templates.description: Extracts the major id from a device id

templates.name: linux_device_minor_id
templates.description: Extracts the minor id from a device id

templates.name: cpu_frequency_counters
templates.description: Counter information for each frequency change for each CPU. Finds each time region where a CPU frequency is constant.

templates.name: cpu_idle_counters
templates.description: Counter information for each idle state change for each CPU. Finds each time region where a CPU idle state is constant.

templates.name: cpu_idle_stats
templates.description: Aggregates cpu idle statistics per core.

templates.name: linux_per_cpu_idle_time_in_state_counters
templates.description: Percentage counter information for sysfs cpuidle states. For each state per cpu, report the incremental time spent in one state, divided by time spent in all states, between two timestamps.

templates.name: linux_cpu_idle_time_in_state_counters
templates.description: Percentage counter information for sysfs cpuidle states. For each state across all CPUs, report the incremental time spent in one state, divided by time spent in all states, between two timestamps.

templates.name: _partition_start
templates.description: Returns the timestamp of the start of the partition that contains the |ts|.

templates.name: _partition_count
templates.description: Returns the number of partitions required to cover all of the trace timestamps.

templates.name: _partitions
templates.description: Returns a table of partitions with first partition containing the TRACE_START() and last one containing TRACE_END().

templates.name: _interval_partitions
templates.description: Partitions any |intervals| table with partitions defined in the |partitions| table.

templates.name: _cpu_avg_utilization_per_period
templates.description: Returns a table of utilization per given period. Utilization is calculated as sum of average utilization of each CPU in each period, which is defined as a multiply of |interval|. For this reason first and last period might have lower then real utilization.

templates.name: cpu_process_utilization_per_period
templates.description: Returns a table of process utilization per given period. Utilization is calculated as sum of average utilization of each CPU in each period, which is defined as a multiply of |interval|. For this reason first and last period might have lower then real utilization.

templates.name: cpu_process_utilization_per_second
templates.description: Returns a table of process utilization per second. Utilization is calculated as sum of average utilization of each CPU in each period, which is defined as a multiply of |interval|. For this reason first and last period might have lower then real utilization.

templates.name: cpu_cycles_per_process
templates.description: Aggregated CPU statistics for each process.

templates.name: cpu_cycles_per_process_in_interval
templates.description: Aggregated CPU statistics for each process in a provided interval. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

templates.name: cpu_process_utilization_in_interval
templates.description: Returns a table with process utilization over a given interval. Utilization is computed as runtime over the duration of the interval, aggregated by UPID. Utilization can be normalized (divide by number of cpus) or unnormalized. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

templates.name: cpu_cycles_per_thread_slice_in_interval
templates.description: CPU cycles per each slice in interval. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

templates.name: cpu_utilization_per_period
templates.description: Returns a table of system utilization per given period. Utilization is calculated as sum of average utilization of each CPU in each period, which is defined as a multiply of |interval|. For this reason first and last period might have lower then real utilization.

templates.name: cpu_utilization_per_second
templates.description: Table with system utilization per second. Utilization is calculated by sum of average utilization of each CPU every second. For this reason first and last second might have lower then real utilization.

templates.name: cpu_cycles
templates.description: Aggregated CPU statistics for whole trace. Results in only one row.

templates.name: cpu_cycles_in_interval
templates.description: Aggregated CPU statistics in a provided interval. Results in one row. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

templates.name: cpu_utilization_in_interval
templates.description: Returns a table of CPU utilization over a given interval. Utilization  is computed as runtime over the duration of the interval. Utilization can be normalized (divide by number of cores) or unnormalized. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

templates.name: cpu_cycles_per_cpu
templates.description: Aggregated CPU statistics for each CPU.

templates.name: cpu_cycles_per_cpu_in_interval
templates.description: Aggregated CPU statistics for each CPU in a provided interval. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

templates.name: cpu_thread_utilization_per_period
templates.description: Returns a table of thread utilization per given period. Utilization is calculated as sum of average utilization of each CPU in each period, which is defined as a multiply of |interval|. For this reason first and last period might have lower then real utilization.

templates.name: cpu_thread_utilization_per_second
templates.description: Returns a table of thread utilization per second. Utilization is calculated as sum of average utilization of each CPU in each period, which is defined as a multiply of |interval|. For this reason first and last period might have lower then real utilization.

templates.name: cpu_cycles_per_thread
templates.description: Aggregated CPU statistics for each thread.

templates.name: cpu_cycles_per_thread_in_interval
templates.description: Aggregated CPU statistics for each thread in a provided interval. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

templates.name: cpu_thread_utilization_in_interval
templates.description: Returns a table of thread utilization over a given interval. Utilization is computed as runtime over the duration of the interval, aggregated by UTID. Utilization can be normalized (divide by number of CPUs) or unnormalized. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

templates.name: cpu_cycles_per_thread_per_cpu
templates.description: Aggregated CPU statistics for each thread per CPU combination. To operate properly this requires sched/sched_switch and power/cpu_frequency ftrace events to be present in the trace.

templates.name: cpu_cycles_per_thread_per_cpu_in_interval
templates.description: Aggregated CPU statistics for each thread per CPU combination in a provided interval. To operate properly this requires sched/sched_switch and power/cpu_frequency ftrace events to be present in the trace. Warning: this query is expensive and might take a long time to execute when joined with a large number of intervals.

templates.name: _get_devfreq_counters

templates.name: regexp_replace_simple
templates.description: Replaces all occurrences of a regular expression with a constant replacement string. Note that there is no way to substitute matching groups into the replacement, and all matching is case-sensitive.

templates.name: base64_encode
templates.description: Encodes bytes into a base64 string.

templates.name: base64_decode
templates.description: Decodes a base64-encoded string into bytes.

templates.name: demangle
templates.description: Demangles a C++ mangled symbol name.

templates.name: regexp_extract
templates.description: Extracts the first match of a regular expression from a string.

templates.name: unhex
templates.description: Converts a hex string (with optional 0x prefix) to an integer.

templates.name: abs_time_str
templates.description: Converts a trace timestamp to an absolute ISO 8601 time string.

templates.name: to_monotonic
templates.description: Converts a trace timestamp to monotonic clock time.

templates.name: to_realtime
templates.description: Converts a trace timestamp to realtime clock time.

templates.name: to_timecode
templates.description: Converts a trace timestamp to a human-readable timecode (HH:MM:SS mmm uuu nnn).

templates.name: ln
templates.description: Computes the natural logarithm of a number.

templates.name: exp
templates.description: Computes e raised to the power of a number.

templates.name: sqrt
templates.description: Computes the square root of a number.

templates.name: metadata
templates.description: Contains metadata about the trace and the system it was collected from.

templates.name: stats
templates.description: Diagnostic stats and parser errors collected at trace time and during import. One row per (key, idx, machine_id, trace_id) combination.

templates.name: _proto_path_to_frame
templates.description: Creates a Stack consisting of one frame for a path in the EXPERIMENTAL_PROTO_PATH table.

templates.name: _proto_path_to_stack
templates.description: Creates a Stack following the parent relations in EXPERIMENTAL_PROTO_PATH table starting at the given path_id.

templates.name: sched_latency_for_running_interval
templates.description: Scheduling latency of running thread states. For each time the thread was running, returns the duration of the runnable state directly before.

templates.name: sched_previous_runnable_on_thread
templates.description: Previous runnable slice on the same thread. For each "Running" thread state finds: - previous "Runnable" (or runnable preempted) state. - previous uninterrupted "Runnable" state with a valid waker thread.

templates.name: sched_state_to_human_readable_string
templates.description: Translates a single-letter scheduling state to a human-readable string.

templates.name: sched_state_io_to_human_readable_string
templates.description: Translates a single-letter scheduling state and IO wait information to a human-readable string.

templates.name: _runnable_state
templates.description: Finds all runnable states that are woken up by a process. We achieve this by checking that the |thread_state.irq_context| value is NOT 1. In otherwords, it is either 0 or NULL. The NULL check is important to support older Android versions. On older versions of Android (<U). We don't have IRQ context information, so this table might contain wakeups from interrupt context, consequently, the wakeup graph generated might not be accurate.

templates.name: _is_fuchsia
templates.description: Defines if the trace is a Fuchsia trace.

templates.name: _is_runnable_state
templates.description: Check whether a given scheduler event is for a task becoming runnable. Linux always has a Waking duration *and* Runnable before each Running duration. Fuchsia only has a Waking duration before the first Running duration, Runnable durations are used if the initial activation's timeslice expires or is interrupted.

templates.name: _first_runnable_state
templates.description: Similar to |_runnable_state| but finds the first runnable state at thread.

templates.name: _sleep_state
templates.description: Finds all sleep states including interruptible (S) and uninterruptible (D).

templates.name: _thread_end_ts
templates.description: Finds the last execution for every thread to end executing_spans without a Sleep.

templates.name: _first_sleep_state
templates.description: Similar to |_sleep_state| but finds the first sleep state in a thread.

templates.name: _wakeup
templates.description: Finds all neighbouring ('Sleeping', 'Runnable') thread_states pairs from the same thread. More succintly, pairs of S[n-1]-R[n] where R is woken by a process context and S is an interruptible or uninterruptible sleep state. This is achieved by joining the |_runnable_state|.ts with the |_sleep_state|.|ts + dur|. With the S-R pairs of a thread, we can re-align to [R-S) intervals with LEADS and LAGS. Given the following thread_states on a thread: S0__|R0__Running0___|S1__|R1__Running1___|S2__|R2__Running2__S2|. We have 3 thread_executing_spans: [R0, S0), [R1, S1), [R2, S2). We define the following markers in this table: prev_id          = R0_id. prev_end_ts      = S0_ts. state            = 'S' or 'D'. blocked_function = <kernel blocking function> id               = R1_id. ts               = R1_ts. end_ts           = S1_ts.

templates.name: _wakeup_map
templates.description: Mapping from running thread state to runnable TODO(zezeozue): Switch to use `sched_previous_runnable_on_thread`.

templates.name: _wakeup_graph
templates.description: Builds the waker and prev relationships for all thread_executing_spans.

templates.name: _intervals_to_roots
templates.description: Converts a table with <ts, dur, utid> columns to a unique set of wakeup roots <id> that completely cover the time intervals.

templates.name: _critical_path_with_depth_by_roots
templates.description: Critical path for the given roots with the chain depth retained. One row per `(root_id, depth, ts, dur, id, parent_id)` blocker frame: `id` is the on-CPU blocker at this depth, `parent_id` is the blocker one level up (the root at depth 0). At a self-wake the woken thread is the depth-N fallback (it is in kernel) and the waker chain layers at depth N+1. `_critical_path_by_roots` collapses these depths to one blocker per `(root_id, ts)`.

templates.name: _critical_path_by_roots
templates.description: Critical path for the given roots, one blocker per `(root_id, ts)`. Pair with `_intervals_to_roots` to compute the path over a sparse time region (e.g. binder transactions) without walking the whole trace.

templates.name: _critical_path_by_intervals
templates.description: Generates the critical path for only the time intervals for the utids given. Currently expensive because of naive interval_intersect implementation. Prefer _critical_paths_by_roots for performance. This is useful for a small set of intervals, e.g app startups in a trace.

templates.name: _thread_executing_span_critical_path
templates.description: Generates the critical path for a given utid over the <ts, dur> interval. The duration of a thread executing span in the critical path is the range between the start of the thread_executing_span and the start of the next span in the critical path.

templates.name: _critical_path_all
templates.description: Critical path rooted at every entry of `_wakeup_graph`, with the on-CPU blocker thread (`utid`) and the root's thread (`root_utid`) joined in. Materialised so the slice-aware tables below can join in id-space.

templates.name: _span_thread_state_view
templates.description: Limited thread_state view that will later be span joined with the |_thread_executing_span_graph|.

templates.name: _span_slice_view
templates.description: Slice projection for SPAN_JOIN with thread_state. Carries `slice_id` only; the slice's name is joined on demand at the leaves of `_critical_path_stack` so the materialised cross-product below does not duplicate every slice's name across every blocker region.

templates.name: _critical_path_thread_state_slice
templates.description: Critical-path × slice cross product, restricted to id-space columns. Slice names are not stored here; `_critical_path_stack` joins `slice` on `slice_id` for the rows it actually returns.

templates.name: _self_view
templates.description: Projection of |_self_sp| in id-space. Slice names are joined on `self_slice_id` at the leaves of `_critical_path_stack`.

templates.name: _critical_path_stack
templates.description: Returns a view of |_self_and_critical_path_sp| unpivoted over the following columns: self thread_state. self blocked_function (if one exists). self process_name (enabled with |enable_process_name|). self thread_name (enabled with |enable_thread_name|). self slice_stack (enabled with |enable_self_slice|). critical_path thread_state. critical_path process_name. critical_path thread_name. critical_path slice_stack (enabled with |enable_critical_path_slice|). running cpu (if one exists). A 'stack' is the group of resulting unpivoted rows sharing the same timestamp.

templates.name: _thread_executing_span_critical_path_stack
templates.description: Critical path stack of thread_executing_spans with the following entities in the critical path stacked from top to bottom: self thread_state, self blocked_function, self process_name, self thread_name, slice stack, critical_path thread_state, critical_path process_name, critical_path thread_name, critical_path slice_stack, running_cpu.

templates.name: _critical_path_graph
templates.description: Returns a pprof aggregation of the stacks in |_critical_path_stack|.

templates.name: _thread_executing_span_critical_path_graph
templates.description: Returns a pprof aggreagation of the stacks in |_thread_executing_span_critical_path_stack|

templates.name: sched_runnable_thread_count
templates.description: The count of runnable threads over time.

templates.name: sched_uninterruptible_sleep_thread_count
templates.description: The count of threads in uninterruptible sleep over time.

templates.name: sched_active_cpu_count
templates.description: The count of active CPUs over time.

templates.name: _get_flattened_thread_state
templates.description: Get the thread state breakdown of a flattened slice from its slice id. This table pivoted and summed for better visualization and aggregation. The concept of a "flat slice" is to take the data in the slice table and remove all notion of nesting. For more information, read the description of _slice_flattened.

templates.name: _get_flattened_thread_state_aggregated
templates.description: Get the thread state breakdown of a flattened slice from slice id. This table pivoted and summed for better visualization and aggragation. The concept of a "flat slice" is to take the data in the slice table and remove all notion of nesting. For more information, read the description of _slice_flattened.

templates.name: sched_time_in_state_for_thread
templates.description: The time a thread spent in each scheduling state during it's lifetime.

templates.name: sched_percentage_of_time_in_state
templates.description: Summary of time spent by thread in each scheduling state, in percentage ([0, 100] ranges). Sum of all states might be smaller than 100, as those values are rounded down.

templates.name: sched_time_in_state_for_thread_in_interval
templates.description: Time the thread spent each state in a given interval. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals. Specifically for any non-trivial subset of thread slices, prefer using `thread_slice_time_in_state` in the `slices.time_in_state` module for this purpose instead.

templates.name: sched_time_in_state_and_cpu_for_thread_in_interval
templates.description: Time the thread spent each state and cpu in a given interval. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

templates.name: sched_time_in_state_for_cpu_in_interval
templates.description: Time spent by CPU in each scheduling state in a provided interval. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

templates.name: sched_with_thread_process
templates.description: View of scheduling slices with extended information. It holds slices with kernel thread scheduling information. These slices are collected when the Linux "ftrace" data source is used with the "sched/switch" and "sched/wakeup*" events enabled. The rows in this table will always have a matching row in the |thread_state| table with |thread_state.state| = 'Running'

templates.name: thread_slice_cpu_time
templates.description: Time each thread slice spent running on CPU. Requires scheduling data to be available in the trace.

templates.name: _slice_flattened
templates.description: The concept of a "flat slice" is to take the data in the slice table and remove all notion of nesting; we do this by projecting every slice in a stack to their ancestor slice, i.e at any point in time, taking the  most specific active slice (i.e. the slice at the bottom of the stack) and representing that as the *only* slice that was running during that period. This concept becomes very useful when you try and linearise a trace and compare it with other traces spanning the same user action; "self time" (i.e. time spent in a slice but *not* any children) is easily computed and span joins with thread state become possible without limiting to only depth zero - slices. Note that, no slices will be generated for intervals without without any slices. As an example, consider the following slice stack: ``` A-------------B. ----C----D----. ``` The flattened slice will be: ``` A----C----D----B. ``` @column slice_id           Id of most active slice. @column ts                 Timestamp when `slice.id` became the most active slice. @column dur                Duration of `slice.id` as the most active slice until the next active slice. @column depth              Depth of `slice.id` in the original stack. @column name               Name of `slice.id`. @column root_id            Id of of the top most slice of the stack. @column track_id           Alias for `slice.track_id`. @column utid               Alias for `thread.utid`. @column tid                Alias for `thread.tid` @column thread_name        Alias for `thread.name`. @column upid               Alias for `process.upid`. @column pid                Alias for `process.pid`. @column process_name       Alias for `process.name`.

templates.name: _slice_following_flow
templates.description: Computes the "reachable" set of slices from the |flows| table, starting from slice ids specified in |source_table|. This provides a more efficient result than with the in-built following_flow operator.

templates.name: _slice_ancestor_and_self
templates.description: Similar to `ancestor_slice`, but returns the slice itself in addition to strict ancestors.

templates.name: _slice_descendant_and_self
templates.description: Similar to `descendant_slice`, but returns the slice itself in addition to strict descendants.

templates.name: _slice_remove_nulls_and_reparent
templates.description: Delete rows from |slice_table| where the |column_name| value is NULL. The |parent_id| of the remaining rows are adjusted to point to the closest ancestor remaining. This keeps the trees as connected as possible, allowing further graph analysis.

templates.name: slice_self_dur
templates.description: For every slice in the `slice` table, computes the "self-duration": the time spent in the slice but *not* spent in any child slices.

templates.name: slice_with_stack_id
templates.description: View that provides stack_id and parent_stack_id for all slices by computing them on-demand. Note: This view computes stack hashes on-demand, which may be slower than the previous C++ implementation.

templates.name: ancestor_slice_by_stack
templates.description: Returns all slices that have the given stack_id, along with their ancestors. The stack_id can be obtained from the slice_with_stack_id view.

templates.name: descendant_slice_by_stack
templates.description: Returns all slices that have the given stack_id, along with their descendants. The stack_id can be obtained from the slice_with_stack_id view.

templates.name: thread_slice_time_in_state
templates.description: For each thread slice, returns the sum of the time it spent in various scheduling states. Requires scheduling data to be available in the trace.

templates.name: thread_slice
templates.description: All thread slices with data about thread, thread track and process.

templates.name: process_slice
templates.description: All process slices with data about process track and process.

templates.name: thread_or_process_slice
templates.description: All the slices in the trace associated to a thread or a process along with contextual information about them (e.g. thread name, process name, tid etc).

templates.name: _jit_code
templates.description: Represents a jitted code snippet. TODO(carlscab): Make public

templates.name: _jit_frame
templates.description: Represents a jitted frame. TODO(carlscab): Make public

templates.name: cpu_profiling_samples
templates.description: Table containing all the timestamped samples of CPU profiling which occurred during the trace. Currently, this table is backed by the following data sources: * Linux perf * Simpleperf proto format * macOS instruments * Chrome CPU profiling * Legacy V8 CPU profiling * Profiling data in Gecko traces

templates.name: cpu_profiling_summary_tree
templates.description: Table summarising the callstacks captured during any CPU profiling which occurred during the trace. Specifically, this table returns a tree containing all the callstacks seen during the trace with `self_count` equal to the number of samples with that frame as the leaf and `cumulative_count` equal to the number of samples with the frame anywhere in the tree. The data sources supported are the same as the `cpu_profiling_samples` table.

templates.name: _to_breakpad_module
templates.description: On Linux/Android perfetto profilers (heapprofd, traced_perf) record the .note.gnu.build-id as build_id. Within Google, this can be used as a lookup key for most cases, but not for Chrome/Webview. Chrome is special and stores symbols indexed by "breadkpad module ID". Breakpad module ID can be derived with the following formula: base::StrCat({module_id->substr(6, 2), module_id->substr(4, 2), module_id->substr(2, 2), module_id->substr(0, 2), module_id->substr(10, 2), module_id->substr(8, 2), module_id->substr(14, 2), module_id->substr(12, 2), module_id->substr(16, 16), "0"}); See also https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:services/tracing/public/cpp/stack_sampling/tracing_sampler_profiler.cc;l=603;drc=cba00174ca338153b9c4f0c31ddbabaac7dd38c7 Note that in SQL SUBSTR() indexes are 1-based, not 0 based.

templates.name: _stacks_symbolization_candidates
templates.description: Enumerates modules and rel_pcs that have no associated symbol information, broken down by caller process.

templates.name: gpu_context
templates.description: Maps GPU graphics context IDs to process and API type. Each row represents a unique graphics context seen in the trace, populated from InternedGraphicsContext data attached to GpuRenderStageEvent packets.

templates.name: metasql_unparenthesize_exprlist
templates.description: Removes the surrounding parentheses from a parenthesised expression list, yielding a bare comma-separated expression list. Typically used when you already have something like `(a, b, c)` but need to splice it into a context (e.g. a `GROUP BY` clause or a `PARTITION BY` clause) that expects an unparenthesised list.

templates.name: _metadata_for_primary_scope
templates.description: A table containing a summary of metadata entries, picking the primary entry for each name (favoring the root trace and machine).

templates.name: _metadata_by_trace
templates.description: A table containing pivoted metadata for each trace in the session.

templates.name: _tree_constraint
templates.description: Creates a filter constraint for tree operations. Example: _tree_constraint('name', '!=', 'skip')

templates.name: _tree_where
templates.description: Combines multiple constraints with AND logic. Example: _tree_where(_tree_constraint('x', '>', 10), _tree_constraint('y', '<', 100))

templates.name: _tree_filter
templates.description: Filters a tree structure using constraints, keeping only nodes that match and reparenting orphans. Implements the filter operation from RFC 0016: removes filtered nodes from the tree and reparents their children to the closest surviving ancestor. All filtering logic happens in C++ via __intrinsic_tree_filter(). Example usage: ``` SELECT * FROM _tree_to_table!( _tree_filter( _tree_from_table!((SELECT * FROM my_tree), (name)), _tree_where(_tree_constraint('name', 'GLOB', '*foo*')) ), (name) ); ```

templates.name: _tree_propagate_down
templates.description: Propagates column values from root toward leaves using BFS. For each spec, a new output column is created by copying the source column and then applying the aggregate operation downward through the tree. For each parent→child edge: output[child] = agg(output[parent], output[child]). Each spec is a string of the form 'AGG(source_col) AS output_col' where AGG is one of SUM, MIN, MAX, FIRST, LAST. Case-insensitive. Example usage: ``` SELECT * FROM _tree_to_table!( _tree_propagate_down( _tree_from_table!((SELECT * FROM calls), (fn, ones)), 'SUM(ones) AS depth' ), (fn, depth) ); ```

templates.name: _tree_from_table_col
templates.description: Helper macro to generate 'col_name', t.col_name pairs for tree_from_table.

templates.name: _tree_from_table
templates.description: Creates a tree structure from a table with id, parent_id, and additional columns. The source table must have columns named 'id' and 'parent_id'. Example usage: ``` SELECT _tree_from_table!( (SELECT id, parent_id, name, value FROM my_table), (name, value) ); ```

templates.name: _tree_to_table_col_select
templates.description: Helper macro to generate column selection for tree_to_table. Maps column index (c4, c5, ...) to column name.

templates.name: _tree_to_table_col_bind
templates.description: Helper macro to generate column binding for tree_to_table.

templates.name: _tree_to_table
templates.description: Converts a tree back to a table. The returned table will have: - `_tree_id`: row index in the tree (0-based) - `_tree_parent_id`: parent row index (NULL for roots) - `id`: original id column - `parent_id`: original parent_id column - Additional columns passed to _tree_from_table! Example usage: ``` SELECT * FROM _tree_to_table!( _tree_from_table!( (SELECT id, parent_id, name FROM my_table), (name) ), (name) ); ```

templates.name: _viz_flamegraph_prepare_filter
templates.description: For each frame in |tab|, returns a row containing the result of running all the filtering operations over that frame's name.

templates.name: _viz_flamegraph_filter_frames
templates.description: Walks the forest from root to leaf and performs the following operations: 1) removes frames which were filtered out 2) make any pivot nodes become the roots 3) computes whether the stack as a whole should be retained or not

templates.name: _viz_flamegraph_accumulate
templates.description: Walks the forest from leaves to root and does the following: 1) removes nodes whose stacks are filtered out 2) computes the cumulative value for each node (i.e. the sum of the self value of the node and all descendants).

templates.name: _viz_flamegraph_upwards_hash
templates.description: Propogates the cumulative value of the pivot nodes to the roots and computes the "fingerprint" of the path.

templates.name: _viz_flamegraph_downwards_hash
templates.description: Computes the "fingerprint" of the path by walking from the laves to the root.

templates.name: _viz_flamegraph_merge_hashes
templates.description: Converts a table of hashes and paretn hashes into ids and parent ids, grouping all hashes together.

templates.name: _viz_flamegraph_local_layout
templates.description: Performs a "layout" of nodes in the flamegraph relative to their siblings.

templates.name: _viz_flamegraph_global_layout
templates.description: Walks the graph from root to leaf, propogating the layout of parents to their children.
